43:0.question: A data engineer runs a COPY INTO command to batch load files from a named stage into a table. The same set of files was already successfully loaded by a previous COPY INTO command into that table. Assuming default options, what happens when the second COPY INTO command runs?
options: The files are reloaded, creating duplicate rows in the target table
The command fails with an error indicating the files were already loaded
The files are skipped and not loaded again, because COPY INTO tracks load metadata to avoid reloading
The command truncates the table and reloads all files
43:0.explanation: By default, COPY INTO <table> maintains load metadata that tracks files that have already been loaded, and it will not load files that have already been loaded (based on file name and checksum) unless the metadata has expired. This prevents accidental duplicate loading, so the files are simply skipped. The command does not error, reload, or truncate. To force a reload, the FORCE = TRUE option must be used, which is not the default behavior.

43:1.question: A data engineer runs a COPY INTO command to load files from a named internal stage into a target table. Several files were already loaded successfully in a previous COPY INTO run. When the engineer runs the same COPY INTO command again without any additional options, what is the default behavior regarding those previously loaded files?
options: The files are reloaded, causing duplicate rows in the target table
The command fails with an error because the files already exist in the load history
The files are skipped because COPY INTO tracks load metadata and does not reload files that were already loaded unchanged
The files are only reloaded if the target table has been truncated since the last load
43:1.explanation: By default, COPY INTO <table> uses load metadata associated with the table to avoid reloading files. Snowflake maintains this metadata for 64 days and skips files that have already been loaded and have not changed, preventing duplicates. Reloading duplicates would require the FORCE = TRUE option. The command does not fail, and skipping is not tied to truncating the table.

44:0.question: A developer wants to unload data from a table into a named internal stage using COPY INTO <location>. They want the output written as a single file rather than being split into multiple files. Which option controls whether Snowflake splits the unloaded output into multiple files?
options: SINGLE
MAX_FILE_SIZE
OVERWRITE
PARTITION BY
44:0.explanation: The SINGLE copy option specifies whether to generate a single file or multiple files. By default SINGLE = FALSE, so Snowflake unloads data into multiple files; setting SINGLE = TRUE produces a single output file. MAX_FILE_SIZE limits the size of each file but does not force a single file. OVERWRITE controls whether existing files are replaced. PARTITION BY is used to partition unloaded data into a directory structure.

46:0.question: A developer runs a PUT command to stage a file but does not specify any named stage or table — they simply want to stage files that only they can access for personal use. Which Snowflake stage type is automatically available for this purpose without requiring any CREATE STAGE statement?
options: A named internal stage referenced by @stage_name
A table stage referenced by @%table_name
The user stage referenced by @~
A named external stage pointing to cloud storage
46:0.explanation: Each user is automatically allocated a personal user stage, referenced with @~, for staging files. It is created by default, cannot be altered or dropped, and is accessible only by that user. A named internal stage must be explicitly created with CREATE STAGE. A table stage (@%table_name) is tied to a specific table and holds files for loading into that table, not for personal use. A named external stage references an external cloud location and must also be explicitly created.

citations.title: Choosing an internal stage for local files

44:1.question: When unloading data using COPY INTO <location>, a team notices their output files all have a .gz extension even though they did not explicitly request compression. Which statement correctly explains the default compression behavior for data unloading?
options: Snowflake never compresses unloaded files unless COMPRESSION is explicitly set
Unloaded files are always compressed with BZIP2 by default
Unloaded files are compressed with gzip by default, and compression can be changed or disabled with the COMPRESSION file format option
Compression only applies when unloading to external stages, not internal stages
44:1.explanation: For data unloading, the default file format compression is gzip, which produces the .gz extension on output files. This can be changed to another supported algorithm (such as bzip2, brotli, zstd) or disabled by setting COMPRESSION = NONE in the file format options. Compression is not off by default, the default is gzip rather than bzip2, and the compression behavior applies regardless of whether the destination is an internal or external stage.

45:0.question: A data engineer wants to view all files currently staged in a named internal stage, including their sizes and last-modified timestamps, before running a COPY INTO to load them. Which command should they use?
options: VALIDATE
GET
LIST
SHOW STAGES
45:0.explanation: The LIST command (alias LS) returns a list of files that have been staged in an internal or external stage, including the file path, size, an MD5 value, and the last-modified timestamp. VALIDATE is used to validate files already loaded by a prior COPY INTO for errors. GET downloads files from a stage to a local directory. SHOW STAGES lists the stage objects themselves, not the files inside a stage.

45:1.question: After running a COPY INTO command that completed, a data engineer wants to review the errors that were encountered during that specific load, without actually loading any additional data. They decide to use the VALIDATE table function. Which statement is TRUE about the VALIDATE function?
options: VALIDATE loads valid rows into the table and only rejects rows containing errors
VALIDATE validates the files loaded in a past COPY INTO execution and returns the errors encountered, without loading any data
VALIDATE can only be run with the VALIDATION_MODE parameter set inside the COPY INTO command
VALIDATE checks staged files for errors before any COPY INTO has been executed against them
45:1.explanation: The VALIDATE table function validates the files that were loaded in a past COPY INTO execution and returns all the errors encountered during that load, rather than just the first error. It does not load any data into the table. VALIDATION_MODE is a separate option used within a COPY INTO statement to dry-run a load; VALIDATE is a table function referencing a prior COPY execution (by job ID or LAST_LOAD), so it requires that a COPY INTO already ran.

46:1.question: A data engineer wants to configure a stage that references files stored in an Amazon S3 bucket, so that COPY INTO commands can load data directly from that external location. Which stage type must be created for this scenario?
options: A named external stage
A table stage
A user stage
A named internal stage
46:1.explanation: A named external stage references data files stored in an external cloud location such as Amazon S3, Google Cloud Storage, or Microsoft Azure. Table stages, user stages, and named internal stages all store files within Snowflake-managed internal storage, not in a customer-owned external bucket.

47:0.question: An organization wants to automatically load new data files into a target table as soon as those files arrive in a cloud storage bucket, without manually issuing COPY commands or maintaining a running warehouse for the load. Which Snowflake feature is designed for this continuous, serverless file ingestion?
options: A scheduled task running COPY INTO on a user-managed warehouse
Snowpipe
Snowpipe Streaming
Bulk data loading with the VALIDATE function
47:0.explanation: Snowpipe enables loading data from files as soon as they are available in a stage, using Snowflake-supplied compute resources (serverless) rather than a user-managed virtual warehouse. A task running COPY INTO would require a user-managed warehouse and runs on a schedule rather than reacting to file arrival. Snowpipe Streaming ingests rows directly via the Ingest SDK rather than loading files. VALIDATE is used to inspect errors from prior COPY executions, not to ingest data.

48:0.question: An application needs to write individual rows into Snowflake tables with very low latency, streaming data directly from the application rather than first writing files to a stage. Which statement accurately describes Snowpipe Streaming?
options: It requires files to be uploaded to an internal stage before rows can be ingested
It uses a running user-managed virtual warehouse to execute COPY INTO for each batch
It writes rows directly into target tables using the Snowflake Ingest SDK, avoiding the need to stage files first
It is only available in Snowflake Enterprise Edition and above
48:0.explanation: Snowpipe Streaming writes rows of data directly into Snowflake tables using the Snowflake Ingest SDK, without requiring files to first be written to a stage — this reduces latency and cost compared to file-based Snowpipe. It does not stage files first, and it does not rely on a user-managed warehouse running COPY INTO. The distractor about Enterprise Edition introduces an edition restriction that is not the defining characteristic described in the documentation.

47:1.question: A team is setting up Snowpipe to ingest files. They are evaluating the two mechanisms Snowflake supports for detecting when new files are available in a stage. Which two methods can trigger a Snowpipe data load? (Select all that apply.)
options: Automating loads using cloud storage event notifications
Calling the Snowpipe REST API endpoints to notify Snowflake of new files
Configuring a resource monitor to trigger the pipe when a credit threshold is reached
Creating a stream on the stage that automatically executes the pipe
Scheduling a serverless task that polls the directory table hourly
47:1.explanation: Snowpipe supports two workflows for detecting new files: automating loads using cloud storage event notifications (auto-ingest), and calling the Snowpipe REST API endpoints to signal that new files are staged. Resource monitors track and limit credit usage but do not trigger pipes. Streams track table/view changes and are not used to trigger a pipe on a stage. Scheduling a polling task is a separate task-based pattern, not one of Snowpipe's built-in triggering mechanisms.

50:0.question: Which of the following file formats are supported by Snowflake for loading semi-structured data into a table? Select all that apply.
options: JSON
Avro
ORC
Parquet
XML
50:0.explanation: Snowflake supports loading semi-structured data from JSON, Avro, ORC, Parquet, and XML file formats. All five are documented as supported semi-structured formats, so every option applies. (Note: XML support for loading is generally available.)

49:0.question: A data engineer is preparing large volumes of data to load into Snowflake using COPY INTO and wants to maximize parallel loading efficiency. According to Snowflake's general file sizing recommendations for loading, what is the recommended approach for compressed data file size?
options: Produce a single very large compressed file of several gigabytes so that one COPY statement handles it in one operation
Aim for compressed data files roughly 100–250 MB in size, splitting very large files into a number of smaller files to distribute the load across compute resources
Keep each compressed file under 1 MB to minimize per-file processing latency
File size has no effect on load parallelism because Snowflake automatically splits any single file across all cluster threads
49:0.explanation: Snowflake recommends aggregating data into compressed files roughly 100–250 MB in size and splitting large files into a greater number of smaller files, which allows the load to be distributed across the servers in a warehouse and enables parallel processing. Option 0 is wrong because a single huge file cannot be parallelized effectively and limits throughput. Option 2 is wrong because too many tiny files increase overhead relative to the actual data processed. Option 3 is wrong because Snowflake does not split an individual file across threads — parallelism is achieved by having multiple appropriately sized files.

51:0.question: A VARIANT column named v contains an object with an array element. An analyst wants to produce one output row for each element of that array so the nested values can be queried as columns. Which Snowflake table function is designed to expand (explode) such nested semi-structured data into multiple rows?
options: PARSE_JSON
OBJECT_KEYS
FLATTEN
ARRAY_AGG
51:0.explanation: FLATTEN is a table function that produces a row for each element of a VARIANT, OBJECT, or ARRAY, effectively exploding nested semi-structured data into rows. PARSE_JSON (A) converts a string to a VARIANT but does not produce rows. OBJECT_KEYS (B) returns the keys of an object as an array, not one row per nested element in the FLATTEN sense. ARRAY_AGG (D) does the opposite—it aggregates values into an array.

49:1.question: A data engineer is preparing large data files for a bulk load using COPY INTO and wants to maximize load performance by taking advantage of parallel processing across the servers in a warehouse. According to Snowflake's general file sizing recommendations, what compressed file size range should the engineer aim for when producing data files?
options: Roughly 10 MB to 100 MB compressed
Roughly 100 MB to 250 MB compressed
Roughly 250 MB or larger, with no upper limit
Under 1 MB compressed to maximize the number of files
49:1.explanation: Snowflake recommends producing data files roughly 100–250 MB (or larger) compressed to optimize the number of parallel operations for a load. Very small files (option D) add disproportionate overhead relative to the data processed, while the 10–100 MB range (option A) is below the recommended target. Option C omits the recommended starting point of about 100 MB and the practical guidance that extremely large files should generally be split; Snowflake explicitly recommends the 100–250 MB (or larger) compressed range as the target.

citations.title: Preparing your data files — File Sizing Best Practices and Limitations

52:0.question: A data engineer needs to upload local CSV files from their workstation to a Snowflake internal stage before running COPY INTO. Which statement about the PUT command is correct?
options: PUT can upload files directly to an external stage located in an Amazon S3 bucket.
PUT automatically compresses files using gzip by default before uploading them to the stage.
PUT can be executed from the Snowsight worksheet interface just like any other SQL statement.
PUT loads the data from the local files directly into the target table in a single step.
52:0.explanation: By default, PUT compresses files using gzip unless AUTO_COMPRESS is set to FALSE. Option A is wrong because PUT does not support uploading to external stages—only internal stages (user, table, or named internal stages). Option C is wrong because PUT is not supported in worksheets/Snowsight UI; it must be run via SnowSQL, the Snowflake CLI, or a supported driver/connector. Option D is wrong because PUT only stages files—loading into a table requires a subsequent COPY INTO command.

52:1.question: A developer uses the GET command to download files from a Snowflake internal stage to a local directory. Which statements accurately describe the behavior and limitations of GET? (Select all that apply.)
options: GET can download files from a named internal stage, a user stage, or a table stage.
GET is supported when executed from the Snowsight worksheet interface.
GET decrypts files that were encrypted during upload as they are downloaded to the local system.
GET can be used to download files from an external stage such as an Azure container.
GET downloads files from the stage to a local directory that must be specified in the command.
52:1.explanation: GET works with internal stages (named, user, and table stages), decrypts files that were encrypted on upload, and requires a local target directory to be specified. Option B is incorrect because GET (like PUT) is not supported in Snowsight worksheets; it must be run through SnowSQL, the Snowflake CLI, or a supported connector/driver. Option D is incorrect because GET does not support external stages—only internal stages.

53:0.question: A table has a standard stream created on it. After several DML operations, a query against the stream returns rows including a row with METADATA$ACTION = 'INSERT' and METADATA$ISUPDATE = TRUE. What does this combination indicate about the underlying change?
options: A new row was inserted into the source table that did not exist before.
A row was deleted from the source table.
The stream is corrupted and must be recreated.
A row was updated; the update is represented as a delete and insert pair, and this is the inserted (new) version of the row.
53:0.explanation: In a standard stream, an UPDATE is represented as a pair of rows: a DELETE (the old value) and an INSERT (the new value), and both carry METADATA$ISUPDATE = TRUE. A row with METADATA$ACTION = 'INSERT' and METADATA$ISUPDATE = TRUE is the new version of an updated row. Option A is wrong because a genuine insert would have METADATA$ISUPDATE = FALSE. Option B is wrong because a delete has METADATA$ACTION = 'DELETE'. Option C is wrong because this is normal, documented stream behavior.

53:1.question: A team wants a stream that tracks only INSERT operations on a source table and does not record any information about updates or deletes, in order to support an efficient extract-only pipeline. Which type of stream should they create?
options: An append-only stream
A standard stream
An insert-only stream on the base table
A read-only stream
53:1.explanation: An append-only stream tracks row inserts only; it does not record delete or update operations, which makes it more performant than a standard stream for insert-only workloads. A standard stream (option B) tracks inserts, updates, and deletes. Insert-only streams (option C) exist but are supported only for streams on external tables and directory tables, not standard base tables. 'Read-only stream' (option D) is not a stream type for base tables—read-only streams apply to streams on shared/secure objects, not a way to capture inserts only.

54:1.question: A data engineer wants a task to run automatically whenever new data arrives in a source table, rather than on a fixed time-based schedule. Which task configuration achieves this in Snowflake?
options: Set the task's SCHEDULE parameter to '1 MINUTE' so it polls the table continuously
Create the task with a stream in its WHEN clause using SYSTEM$STREAM_HAS_DATA so it only executes when the stream has new data
Configure the task as a triggered task by omitting SCHEDULE and referencing a stream, so it runs when the stream detects change data
Attach a Snowpipe to the task so the task fires on each file load event
54:1.explanation: Triggered tasks execute in response to changes in an underlying stream rather than on a fixed schedule. A triggered task is created without a SCHEDULE and references a stream; Snowflake runs it when the stream detects new change data. Setting a 1-minute SCHEDULE is still time-based polling (and consumes credits regardless of data), not truly event-driven. The WHEN/SYSTEM$STREAM_HAS_DATA clause is a valid optimization but with a SCHEDULE it is still a scheduled task that merely skips execution — it is not the mechanism for a triggered task. Snowpipe cannot 'fire' a task directly.

55:0.question: A team defines a Dynamic Table with a query joining several source tables and sets a TARGET_LAG of '20 minutes'. What does TARGET_LAG specify for this Dynamic Table?
options: The maximum amount of time the Dynamic Table's content can be behind updates to its base tables
The fixed interval at which the Dynamic Table is fully rebuilt from scratch
The minimum time a query result is cached before the table refreshes
The amount of time before the Dynamic Table is automatically suspended for inactivity
55:0.explanation: TARGET_LAG defines how out-of-date the Dynamic Table is allowed to be relative to its base tables — the maximum acceptable lag between updates to the sources and the Dynamic Table's contents. Snowflake schedules automatic refreshes to keep the data within this lag. It is not a fixed full-rebuild interval (refreshes are incremental when possible), not a query result cache setting, and not an inactivity suspension timer.

56:0.question: Why does Snowflake recommend using a storage integration to access an external cloud storage location (such as an Amazon S3 bucket) instead of passing credentials directly in a stage or COPY statement?
options: A storage integration encrypts the data files at rest, which credential-based access does not
A storage integration avoids the need to supply explicit cloud credentials (such as secret keys) when creating stages or loading/unloading data
A storage integration is required in order to load semi-structured data like JSON or Parquet
A storage integration automatically converts external files into Snowflake's internal micro-partition format on load
56:0.explanation: A storage integration is a Snowflake object that stores a generated identity and access management entity, allowing users to avoid supplying explicit cloud provider credentials (like AWS secret keys or SAS tokens) when creating external stages or loading/unloading data. It does not itself encrypt data at rest, is not required for any particular file format, and does not convert external files into micro-partitions.

50:1.question: A team receives data files in the Delimited-text CSV format as well as several other formats. They want to load the semi-structured formats into a VARIANT column. Which of the listed formats is NOT one of the semi-structured file formats Snowflake supports for loading?
options: Avro
Parquet
CSV
ORC
50:1.explanation: CSV (delimited text) is a structured/flat file format, not a semi-structured format. The semi-structured formats Snowflake supports for loading are JSON, Avro, ORC, Parquet, and XML. Avro (A), Parquet (B), and ORC (D) are all supported semi-structured formats; CSV is loaded as delimited text into relational columns, not into a VARIANT via the semi-structured loading path.

citations.title: Overview of supported data file formats

51:1.question: When using the FLATTEN table function in a query, which clause is required so that FLATTEN can reference a column from a table in the same FROM clause (for example, to flatten a VARIANT column of an existing table)?
options: The LATERAL keyword (or a comma join) combining the table with FLATTEN
The MATCH_RECOGNIZE clause applied to the table
The PIVOT clause referencing the VARIANT column
The QUALIFY clause filtering the flattened rows
51:1.explanation: To flatten a column from another table in the same query, FLATTEN must be joined laterally to that table—typically written using the LATERAL keyword or an equivalent comma (cross join) in the FROM clause—so FLATTEN can reference the table's column on each row. MATCH_RECOGNIZE (B) is for row pattern matching, PIVOT (C) rotates rows into columns, and QUALIFY (D) filters based on window functions; none of these enable FLATTEN to reference another table's column.

54:0.question: A data engineer wants a task to automatically run a stored procedure only when a specific stream contains new change data, and to skip execution (without consuming compute) when the stream is empty. Which approach correctly achieves this behavior?
options: Configure the task with a WHEN clause that uses the SYSTEM$STREAM_HAS_DATA function to check the stream before running.
Set the task's SCHEDULE to '1 MINUTE' and rely on the stream to pause the warehouse automatically when there is no data.
Create the task without a schedule and manually call EXECUTE TASK each time the stream receives data.
Add a RESOURCE_MONITOR to the task so it only runs when credits are available.
54:0.explanation: A task can include a WHEN clause with the SYSTEM$STREAM_HAS_DATA boolean function; when it evaluates to FALSE the scheduled task run is skipped without initiating compute, avoiding unnecessary credit consumption. Option B is wrong because a schedule alone does not skip runs based on stream contents, and streams do not manage warehouse suspension. Option C is technically possible for manual runs but does not provide automatic conditional execution and is not the documented pattern for this requirement. Option D is wrong because resource monitors control credit quotas for warehouses, not conditional task execution based on stream data.

60:0.question: A user runs a query and it returns instantly from the query result cache. Which of the following conditions must be true for Snowflake to reuse the persisted result of a previously executed query?
options: The new query must run on the same virtual warehouse that produced the original result
The underlying data (micro-partitions) accessed by the query must not have changed, and the query text must match exactly
The original query must have been executed within the last 90 days without any subsequent access
The role executing the new query must be the same role that ran the original query
60:0.explanation: Snowflake reuses persisted query results only when the new query syntactically matches the previous query and the table data contributing to the result has not changed. Option A is wrong because the result cache is maintained by the cloud services layer and is independent of the virtual warehouse — no warehouse is needed to retrieve a cached result. Option C is wrong because results are retained for 24 hours (extended up to 31 days maximum with repeated reuse), not 90 days. Option D is wrong because result reuse requires the same role only in the sense that the querying user must have the necessary privileges; identical role identity is not a stated requirement, and data-change/query-match are the core conditions.

59:0.question: A user re-runs an identical SELECT query 5 minutes after the first execution. No underlying data has changed and no DML has occurred on the queried tables. Snowflake returns results almost instantly without starting or using the virtual warehouse compute. Which caching layer is responsible for this behavior?
options: The warehouse (local disk) cache
The metadata cache in the cloud services layer
The query result cache in the cloud services layer
The remote disk cache in the storage layer
59:0.explanation: The query result cache holds the results of previously executed queries in the cloud services layer. If the same query is re-run and the results are still valid (no changes to underlying data, and other conditions met), Snowflake returns the cached result without using any virtual warehouse compute. The warehouse (local disk) cache stores raw table data on the SSD of an active warehouse and still requires the warehouse to run. The metadata cache serves object statistics but does not return full query results. The storage layer is remote persistent storage, not a query-result cache.

56:1.question: A cloud administrator is configuring a Snowflake storage integration for an Amazon S3 bucket. Which statement accurately describes how a storage integration works with respect to cloud credentials?
options: The storage integration stores the S3 access key and secret key encrypted within Snowflake so they can be reused across multiple stages
A storage integration is a named, account-level object that stores a generated identity and access management (IAM) entity, avoiding the need to supply explicit cloud credentials when creating stages
The storage integration passes the cloud credentials directly into each COPY statement at runtime for auditing purposes
A storage integration must be created separately in each schema where an external stage will reference it
56:1.explanation: A storage integration is a Snowflake account-level object that stores a generated identity and access management (IAM) user for a specified cloud provider, along with an optional set of allowed or blocked storage locations. This lets users create external stages and reference cloud storage without supplying explicit credentials (secret keys, SAS tokens, etc.). It does not store access/secret keys inside Snowflake, and it does not pass credentials into COPY statements. Because it is an account-level object, it is created once and can be referenced by stages across the account rather than being created per schema.

58:0.question: A user runs a COPY INTO command to load a batch of files and wants the entire load operation to fail and roll back if any single row in any file cannot be parsed. Which ON_ERROR option should be specified to achieve this behavior?
options: ON_ERROR = ABORT_STATEMENT
ON_ERROR = CONTINUE
ON_ERROR = SKIP_FILE
ON_ERROR = SKIP_FILE_10%
58:0.explanation: ABORT_STATEMENT aborts the entire load operation if any error is encountered in any data file. CONTINUE loads valid rows and skips only the erroneous rows, allowing the load to proceed. SKIP_FILE skips only the file containing the error but continues with other files. SKIP_FILE_10% skips a file only when the number of errors in it exceeds 10%. Note that ABORT_STATEMENT is the default for bulk COPY INTO commands.

48:1.question: An architect is comparing billing models before choosing between file-based Snowpipe (auto-ingest) and Snowpipe Streaming (using the Ingest SDK) for a high-frequency row ingestion workload. How is Snowpipe Streaming billed?
options: By the number of files loaded, with a per-file overhead charge similar to file-based Snowpipe
By throughput, calculated as credits per uncompressed GB of data ingested
By per-second usage of a dedicated virtual warehouse that the client must provision
By the number of API calls made to the Ingest SDK, regardless of data volume
48:1.explanation: Snowpipe Streaming uses a throughput-based billing model calculated by credits per uncompressed GB of data ingested. It does not require the user to provision or run a virtual warehouse, and it does not charge per file (file-based Snowpipe charges based on files/compute resources managed by Snowflake, including a per-file overhead). Snowpipe Streaming is not billed per API call. This throughput model reflects the fact that Snowpipe Streaming writes rows directly rather than loading staged files.

58:1.question: A COPY INTO <table> statement is executed with ON_ERROR = SKIP_FILE_5. During loading, one data file contains 3 rows that fail to parse, and another data file contains 8 rows that fail to parse. What is the effect of this ON_ERROR setting on these two files?
options: The first file is skipped and the second file is loaded, because 3 is below the threshold
Both files are loaded in full because only errors above 5% are skipped
The first file is loaded (3 errors, below the threshold of 5) and the second file is skipped (8 errors, meets or exceeds 5)
The entire statement aborts because at least one file exceeds the error count of 5
58:1.explanation: SKIP_FILE_num skips a file when the number of error rows in that file is equal to or greater than the specified number. With SKIP_FILE_5, the first file has 3 errors (below 5) so it is loaded, and the second file has 8 errors (at or above 5) so it is skipped. It does not abort the whole statement (that is ABORT_STATEMENT behavior), and the percentage interpretation applies only to the SKIP_FILE_num% variant, not the plain integer form.

61:0.question: A data engineering team runs large, complex analytical queries that individually take a long time to complete because they process very large data volumes. They want each individual query to run faster. Which action is the appropriate approach?
options: Enable a multi-cluster warehouse to add more clusters when the query runs
Increase the number of maximum clusters on the existing multi-cluster warehouse
Resize the warehouse to a larger size to provide more compute resources per query
Set the scaling policy of the warehouse to Economy
61:0.explanation: Resizing a warehouse to a larger size (scaling up) provides more compute resources (servers) that can improve the performance of individual large or complex queries. Options A and B describe scaling out with multi-cluster warehouses, which addresses concurrency — handling more simultaneous queries/users — not the speed of a single query. Option D (Economy scaling policy) governs how additional clusters start/stop for concurrency and does not improve individual query performance.

62:0.question: A multi-cluster warehouse is configured in Auto-scale mode. Which statement correctly describes the difference between the Standard and Economy scaling policies?
options: The Economy policy starts additional clusters immediately when queuing occurs, while Standard waits to conserve credits
The Standard policy is only available in Enterprise Edition, while Economy is available in all editions

The Standard policy prevents/minimizes queuing by starting clusters as soon as needed, while the Economy policy conserves credits by favoring keeping running clusters fully loaded before starting another
Both policies start additional clusters identically but differ only in how quickly clusters are suspended
62:0.explanation: With the Standard scaling policy, Snowflake starts additional clusters immediately when queries queue or the system detects one is needed, minimizing queuing. The Economy policy conserves credits by keeping running clusters more fully loaded, only starting another cluster if the system estimates there is enough query load to keep it busy for several minutes — which can allow some queuing. Option A reverses the two policies. Option B is incorrect; multi-cluster warehouses (both policies) require Enterprise Edition or higher, and there is no edition split between the two policies. Option D is wrong because the policies differ in how additional clusters are started, not solely suspension behavior.

citations.title: Multi-cluster Warehouses — Setting the Scaling Policy

62:1.question: A multi-cluster warehouse is configured with a minimum of 1 cluster and a maximum of 5 clusters. The workload experiences frequent, sustained spikes in concurrent queries, and the team wants to prioritize immediately starting additional clusters to avoid any query queuing, even if it means clusters may run less fully loaded. Which scaling policy should be selected to meet this goal?
options: Economy
Standard
Maximized
Auto-scale
62:1.explanation: The Standard scaling policy prevents/minimizes queuing by favoring starting additional clusters as soon as either a query is queued or the system detects one more query than the currently running clusters can execute. This prioritizes performance over conserving credits, matching the goal of avoiding queuing. The Economy policy conserves credits by keeping running clusters fully loaded and only starts a cluster if the system estimates enough queued query load to keep it busy for at least 6 minutes, which allows more queuing. 'Maximized' is not a scaling policy — it describes a warehouse mode where minimum equals maximum clusters (all clusters always run), but here min (1) does not equal max (5), so it is auto-scaling. 'Auto-scale' is a mode of operation, not a named scaling policy.

63:1.question: An administrator resizes a running warehouse from Medium to Large while several queries are executing and additional queries are queued. Which statement accurately describes the effect of this resize? (Select all that apply.)
options: Currently executing queries continue to use the compute resources they were allocated when they started.
Queued queries and newly submitted queries can take advantage of the additional resources from the larger size.
All executing queries are terminated and rerun on the larger warehouse.
The resize instantly reduces the execution time of queries that are already in progress.
The additional compute resources become available once the provisioning of the larger size completes.
63:1.explanation: When a running warehouse is resized, queries already executing keep the resources they had when they started (option A). The larger size benefits only queued and subsequently submitted queries (option B), and those additional resources are usable once provisioning of the new size completes (option E). The resize does not terminate or rerun in-progress queries (option C is false), and it does not speed up queries already running (option D is false), since those queries retain their original resources.

61:1.question: An analytics team notices that during peak business hours many concurrent user queries are queuing on a single warehouse, even though each individual query completes quickly. The goal is to reduce query queuing without over-provisioning during off-peak hours. Which solution best addresses this scenario?
options: Increase the warehouse size from Medium to X-Large so each query has more compute
Configure the warehouse as a multi-cluster warehouse with an appropriate minimum and maximum number of clusters
Enable the query result cache so repeated queries return instantly
Set a resource monitor to suspend the warehouse when queuing occurs
61:1.explanation: Query queuing caused by high concurrency is addressed by scaling out — configuring a multi-cluster warehouse so additional clusters start automatically as concurrency increases and shut down when demand drops, avoiding over-provisioning off-peak. Option A (scaling up) improves individual query performance but does not directly resolve concurrency-driven queuing. Option C only helps identical repeated queries and does not address diverse concurrent workloads. Option D would suspend compute and worsen the problem rather than serve more concurrent queries.

63:0.question: A large query is actively running on a warehouse when a user issues an ALTER WAREHOUSE command to increase the warehouse size. What happens to the currently running query?
options: The running query is immediately cancelled and must be resubmitted to use the larger size.
The running query is paused and automatically resumes on the resized warehouse using the additional compute.
The running query continues to run on the original compute resources and is not affected by the resize; the additional resources are used for queued and future queries.
The running query is automatically restarted from the beginning using the additional compute resources.
63:0.explanation: Resizing a warehouse only affects queries that are queued and/or newly submitted after the resize completes. Queries that are already running when the resize occurs continue to execute using the compute resources allocated when they started; they are not cancelled, paused, or restarted. This is why resizing does not accelerate a query already in progress.

64:0.question: A table storing several years of transactional data is frequently filtered on a date column in query WHERE clauses. A clustering key is defined on that date column. How does this clustering key improve query performance?
options: It creates a secondary index structure that Snowflake traverses to locate matching rows.
It physically co-locates data with similar values so that Snowflake can prune (skip scanning) micro-partitions that do not contain data matching the filter.
It compresses the date column more aggressively, reducing the total storage the query must read.
It caches the filtered result set in the cloud services layer so repeated queries return instantly.
64:0.explanation: A clustering key organizes (co-locates) rows with similar clustering-key values into the same micro-partitions. Because Snowflake stores metadata (such as min/max values) for each micro-partition, well-clustered data allows the query optimizer to prune — skip scanning — micro-partitions that cannot contain values matching the filter predicate, reducing the amount of data scanned. Snowflake does not use traditional secondary indexes (option A). Clustering is about organizing data for pruning, not about compression (option C) or result caching (option D).

64:1.question: A data engineer is evaluating whether to define a clustering key on a table. Which of the following statements about clustering keys and micro-partition pruning are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: Clustering keys are generally most effective on very large tables where the amount of data queried and the table size are large enough that clustering benefits outweigh the cost of maintaining the clustering.
Once a clustering key is defined, Snowflake automatically maintains the clustering of the table over time as DML operations occur (automatic clustering).
Defining a clustering key on every column of a table is recommended to maximize pruning across all possible filter predicates.
A well-chosen clustering key improves pruning when queries filter or join on the clustering key columns.
Clustering keys eliminate the need for micro-partitions, storing the entire table as a single contiguous file.
64:1.explanation: Clustering keys are most beneficial on very large tables where clustering benefits outweigh maintenance costs (option A). Snowflake automatically maintains clustering through Automatic Clustering once a clustering key is defined (option B). A well-chosen clustering key improves pruning for queries that filter or join on the clustering-key columns (option D). Defining a clustering key on every column is not recommended — Snowflake recommends a small number of columns (typically up to 3 or 4) because too many columns can add maintenance cost without benefit (option C is false). Clustering does not eliminate micro-partitions; Snowflake always stores table data in micro-partitions (option E is false).

65:1.question: An organization has enabled Automatic Clustering on several large tables. The finance team wants to understand how they will be charged for this feature. Which statement accurately describes the cost model for Automatic Clustering?
options: Automatic Clustering is free of charge and included with all Snowflake editions at no cost.
Automatic Clustering consumes credits from the virtual warehouse used to run queries against the clustered table.
Automatic Clustering incurs only storage costs, with no compute charges.
Automatic Clustering uses Snowflake-managed serverless compute resources, and credits are billed based on that consumption.
65:1.explanation: Automatic Clustering uses Snowflake-managed (serverless) compute resources to perform reclustering, and customers are billed credits for that serverless compute consumption. It is not free, it does not draw from a user's virtual warehouse (which is why no warehouse needs to be running for reclustering to occur), and the cost is compute-based, not storage-only.

67:0.question: A warehouse frequently runs queries with large table scans where the scanning portion of the work occasionally exceeds the warehouse's compute capacity, causing those queries to run slowly compared to typical workloads. The team wants to speed up these outlier queries without permanently increasing the warehouse size. Which Snowflake feature should they enable?
options: Multi-cluster warehouse mode to add more clusters for concurrency.
Automatic Clustering to reorganize the underlying micro-partitions.
The Query Acceleration Service, which offloads portions of eligible queries to serverless compute.
The Search Optimization Service to reduce the number of micro-partitions scanned.
67:0.explanation: The Query Acceleration Service can accelerate parts of the query workload — particularly large scans and filtering — by offloading eligible portions of a query to serverless compute, which is ideal for queries that outsize the warehouse's capacity. Multi-cluster warehouses address concurrency (many simultaneous queries), not the compute demand of an individual large-scan query. Automatic Clustering reorganizes data by clustering key but does not add compute for a scan. Search Optimization accelerates selective point lookups, not large scans.

55:1.question: A data engineer creates a Dynamic Table without explicitly setting the REFRESH_MODE parameter (leaving it at the default of AUTO). What does the AUTO refresh mode do with respect to whether the table refreshes incrementally or by full recompute?
options: It re-evaluates the choice between incremental and full refresh on every scheduled refresh, always picking whichever is cheaper for that cycle.
It resolves to either INCREMENTAL or FULL refresh once when the Dynamic Table is created, based on the query, and that resolved mode remains fixed for the table.
It forces every refresh to be a full recompute of the entire query result to guarantee correctness.
It requires the table to be manually refreshed and never performs automatic refreshes.
55:1.explanation: With REFRESH_MODE = AUTO (the default), Snowflake determines at creation time whether the Dynamic Table can be refreshed incrementally; if incremental refresh is not supported for the query, it uses full refresh. This decision is made once and the resolved refresh mode does not change afterward. Option A describes per-cycle switching, which is not how AUTO behaves. Option C is wrong because AUTO uses incremental refresh when the query supports it, not always full. Option D is wrong because AUTO does not disable automatic refreshes — TARGET_LAG still governs scheduled refreshes.

66:0.question: A team frequently runs queries with highly selective equality predicates that return a small number of rows from a very large table, but these point-lookup queries are slow because they scan many micro-partitions. Which Snowflake feature is specifically designed to improve the performance of this type of query?
options: The Search Optimization Service.
The Query Acceleration Service.
Automatic Clustering.
A materialized view over the base table.
66:0.explanation: The Search Optimization Service is designed to significantly improve the performance of certain types of queries on tables, including selective point-lookup queries that use equality or IN predicates. The Query Acceleration Service targets large scans and heavy filtering/aggregation workloads, not selective point lookups. Automatic Clustering reorganizes data by a clustering key but is not the purpose-built feature for point lookups. A materialized view stores precomputed results but is not the feature specifically designed for accelerating selective point lookups.

60:1.question: A query previously returned results that were persisted in the query result cache. On a subsequent run, an otherwise identical query does NOT reuse the persisted result. Which of the following conditions can prevent Snowflake from reusing a persisted query result? (Select all that apply.)
options: The query includes a function that must be evaluated at execution time, such as one that generates a random value.
The underlying data (or micro-partitions) making up the table has changed since the result was persisted.
The query uses an external function.
The role running the query is different, even though it has all necessary privileges on the referenced objects.
The persisted result was generated less than 24 hours ago.
60:1.explanation: Snowflake's documentation states that a persisted result is only reused if the new query does not include functions that must be evaluated at execution time (non-deterministic/non-reusable functions such as RANDOM), the table data contributing to the result has not changed, and the query does not use external functions, among other conditions. Options A, B, and C match documented conditions that prevent reuse. Option D is incorrect because the role does not need to match — result reuse does not depend on the role being identical (though privileges and table access must be sufficient). Option E is incorrect: results being recent (within retention) supports reuse rather than preventing it; results are retained for 24 hours (extendable up to 31 days), so being under 24 hours old does not prevent reuse.

67:1.question: A data engineer notices that several ad-hoc queries scanning very large tables run slowly on a medium warehouse, with the Query Profile showing that most execution time is spent on table scans and filtering. They want to speed these queries up without permanently resizing the warehouse. Which statement about the Query Acceleration Service (QAS) is correct?
options: QAS is enabled per user session and automatically resizes the underlying virtual warehouse to a larger size for the duration of the query
QAS offloads portions of eligible query workloads, such as large scans and filtering, to shared serverless compute resources that can be added dynamically
QAS caches the full result set of large scans in the cloud services layer so that repeated scans of the same table are eliminated
QAS requires the warehouse to be configured as a multi-cluster warehouse before it can accelerate any query
67:1.explanation: The Query Acceleration Service accelerates parts of a query workload by offloading portions of the work — such as large table scans and filtering operations — to serverless compute resources that Snowflake provisions and shares, allowing the workload to scale beyond the warehouse's own compute. It does not resize the virtual warehouse (that would be scaling up), it is not the result cache, and it does not require a multi-cluster warehouse; QAS is enabled on a warehouse via ENABLE_QUERY_ACCELERATION.

59:1.question: A virtual warehouse maintains a cache of data used to process queries while the warehouse is running. Which statement correctly describes what happens to this warehouse cache when the warehouse is suspended and then later resumed?
options: The cache persists through suspension and is fully available immediately when the warehouse resumes.
The cache is dropped when the warehouse is suspended, which can impact the performance of the first queries after it resumes.
The cache is automatically migrated to the cloud services layer during suspension and restored on resume.
The cache is preserved only if the warehouse is suspended for less than 5 minutes.
59:1.explanation: Snowflake documentation states that a running warehouse maintains a cache of table data, and this cache is dropped when the warehouse is suspended, which may result in slower initial performance for queries after the warehouse is resumed until the cache is rebuilt. Option A is incorrect because the cache does not persist through suspension. Option C is incorrect — the cache is not migrated to the cloud services layer. Option D is incorrect; there is no 5-minute preservation threshold for the warehouse cache.

65:0.question: A data engineer defines a clustering key on a large table using ALTER TABLE ... CLUSTER BY. As DML operations continuously add and modify rows, they want the table's data to remain well-organized according to the clustering key without any manual intervention. Which Snowflake capability meets this requirement?
options: The engineer must periodically run a manual RECLUSTER command during off-peak hours to maintain clustering.
Automatic Clustering, a serverless background service that continuously monitors and reclusters the table as needed.
A dedicated virtual warehouse owned by the engineer must be assigned to perform the reclustering.
Search Optimization Service must be enabled on the table to keep it clustered.
65:0.explanation: Automatic Clustering is the Snowflake service that seamlessly and continuously manages reclustering of clustered tables in the background as needed, using Snowflake-managed serverless compute rather than a user-managed warehouse. There is no manual RECLUSTER command that users must run periodically, and no user warehouse needs to be assigned. Search Optimization Service is an unrelated feature for accelerating point lookups, not for maintaining clustering.

66:1.question: A database administrator wants to use the Search Optimization Service to accelerate queries on a large table. Which of the following are query types or predicates that the Search Optimization Service can help accelerate? (Select all that apply.)
options: Equality and IN predicates used for point lookups.
Substring and regular expression searches using predicates such as LIKE, ILIKE, and RLIKE.
Queries that predicate on VARIANT, OBJECT, and ARRAY columns.
Queries that predicate on geospatial columns using geospatial functions.
Full-table aggregation queries that scan and sum every row in the table.
66:1.explanation: The Search Optimization Service supports several query workloads: selective point-lookup queries using equality and IN predicates, substring and regular-expression searches (LIKE, ILIKE, RLIKE), queries on VARIANT/OBJECT/ARRAY (semi-structured) columns, and queries on geospatial columns using geospatial functions. It does not accelerate full-table aggregation queries that must scan and process all rows — those are not selective and do not benefit from search optimization.

68:0.question: An analyst repeatedly runs an aggregation query over a very large, infrequently changing base table and wants the results to be precomputed and automatically kept up to date as the base table changes. Which Snowflake object best satisfies this requirement?
options: A standard (non-materialized) view, which stores the query results on disk for fast retrieval
A temporary table populated by a scheduled task that re-runs the aggregation each hour
A materialized view, whose results are precomputed, stored, and automatically maintained by Snowflake as the base table changes
A cached result stored in the cloud services layer that persists for up to 31 days
68:0.explanation: A materialized view stores precomputed results of a query and Snowflake automatically maintains (refreshes) those results as the underlying base table data changes, making it ideal for repeated queries over large, slowly changing tables. A standard view does not store results — it re-executes the query each time. A task-populated temporary table is not auto-maintained by Snowflake's background service and requires manual scheduling. The 31-day retention describes the result cache, not a persisted precomputed object.

69:0.question: A team runs a filtered query against a large table that is well-organized by date. In the Query Profile, the TableScan operator shows 'Partitions scanned: 12' and 'Partitions total: 2,000'. What does this indicate about how Snowflake processed the query?
options: Snowflake had to load all 2,000 micro-partitions into the warehouse cache before filtering out the 12 relevant ones
Snowflake used micro-partition pruning to eliminate the vast majority of partitions, scanning only 12 of 2,000 based on partition min/max metadata
The table needs to be manually re-partitioned because scanning only 12 partitions indicates a corrupted clustering key
Snowflake scanned 12 partitions because the warehouse was too small to process all 2,000 at once
The 12 scanned partitions represent the result cache rather than actual table data
69:0.explanation: Snowflake maintains metadata (including min/max value ranges) for each micro-partition. During query pruning, Snowflake uses this metadata to skip micro-partitions that cannot contain rows matching the query's filter, so it scanned only 12 of the 2,000 total partitions — an example of effective pruning. It does not need to load all partitions first, the ratio does not indicate corruption, pruning is unrelated to warehouse size, and scanned partitions are actual table data, not the result cache.

citations.title: Understanding Snowflake Table Structures — Micro-partitions & Query Pruning

70:0.question: A data engineer notices in the Query Profile that a large join operation shows significant 'Bytes spilled to local storage' and 'Bytes spilled to remote storage'. Which of the following is the most likely cause and an appropriate remediation?
options: The query is reading from a materialized view, so the fix is to drop the materialized view
The warehouse does not have enough memory to process the operation, so increasing the warehouse size can reduce or eliminate spilling
The result cache has expired, so the fix is to enable the result cache
The table is not clustered, so the only fix is to define a clustering key
70:0.explanation: Spilling to local (and then remote) storage occurs when an operation cannot fit its intermediate data in the warehouse's available memory, forcing data to spill to disk (local) and, if that is exhausted, to remote storage. Increasing the warehouse size provides more memory and compute, which can reduce or eliminate spilling. Materialized views, the result cache, and clustering are unrelated to memory pressure that causes spilling.

69:1.question: Which statements accurately describe how Snowflake stores and uses micro-partition metadata for query pruning? (Select all that apply.)
options: Snowflake automatically records metadata such as the range of values (min/max) for each column within each micro-partition
Query pruning uses this metadata to skip micro-partitions that cannot satisfy the query's filter predicates
Micro-partitions are created and managed manually by the user by specifying partition boundaries at table creation
Pruning is more effective when the columns used in filter predicates are correlated with how the data is physically ordered/clustered
Each micro-partition contains a fixed 1 GB of uncompressed data
69:1.explanation: Snowflake automatically captures per-micro-partition metadata, including the range of values for each column, and uses it to prune (skip) partitions that cannot match a query's predicates (both correct). Pruning is more effective when filter columns align with the physical clustering/ordering of data (correct). Micro-partitions are created and managed automatically by Snowflake, not defined manually with boundaries. Micro-partitions contain roughly 50–500 MB of uncompressed data (stored compressed), not a fixed 1 GB.

70:1.question: Which of the following statements about query spilling in Snowflake are true? (Select all that apply.)
options: Spilling to remote storage generally has a greater negative impact on query performance than spilling to local storage
Spilling occurs when there is not enough memory to hold intermediate operation results, forcing data to be written to disk
Processing data in smaller batches or reducing the amount of data processed can help avoid spilling
Spilling to disk always improves query performance because disk access is faster than memory
Spilling only affects INSERT statements and never affects SELECT queries
70:1.explanation: Spilling happens when a warehouse runs out of memory for an operation and must write intermediate data to local disk, and then to remote storage if local disk fills up; remote spilling is slower and more damaging to performance than local spilling. Reducing data volume or processing in smaller batches helps avoid spilling. Spilling never improves performance (disk is slower than memory), and it affects query processing broadly, not only INSERTs.

71:1.question: Which statement correctly distinguishes a scalar UDF from a user-defined table function (UDTF) in Snowflake?
options: A scalar UDF returns a single value for each input row, while a UDTF returns a set of rows for each input row
A scalar UDF can only be written in SQL, while a UDTF can only be written in JavaScript
A scalar UDF returns multiple rows, while a UDTF returns a single scalar value
A scalar UDF must be called in the FROM clause, while a UDTF can only appear in the SELECT list
71:1.explanation: A scalar function returns a single value for each input row, whereas a table function (UDTF) returns a tabular value (zero or more rows) for each input row and is referenced in the FROM clause. Both scalar UDFs and UDTFs can be written in multiple languages (SQL, JavaScript, Python, Java, Scala), so the language restriction is false. The remaining options invert the return-shape and calling-location behaviors of the two function types.

71:0.question: A developer needs to create a user-defined function that, given an input, returns a set of rows that can be joined against other tables in a query. Which type of user-defined function should be used?
options: A scalar UDF, because scalar UDFs return one row per input row that can be joined
A stored procedure, because stored procedures return tabular result sets that join naturally in queries
A user-defined table function (UDTF), because it returns a set of rows (a table) that can be used in the FROM clause
A secure view, because views are the only object type that can return multiple rows
71:0.explanation: A UDTF (user-defined table function) returns a tabular value—zero, one, or many rows—and is invoked in the FROM clause, often with LATERAL, so its output can be joined against other tables. A scalar UDF returns a single value per invocation, not a set of rows. Stored procedures are designed for procedural logic and are not invoked directly in the FROM clause to produce joinable tabular output the way UDTFs are. Views are not user-defined functions.

57:1.question: A data engineer plans to connect Snowflake to a Git repository. To create the Git repository object, Snowflake needs a way to reference the allowed repository origins (and, for private repositories, the stored credentials). Which object type is created to define the allowed Git repository origins that a Git repository object can use?
options: A notification integration
A network policy
An external access integration
An API integration
57:1.explanation: To set up Git integration, an API integration is created that specifies the allowed Git repository origins (API_ALLOWED_PREFIXES), and this API integration is referenced when creating the Git repository object. A notification integration is used for messaging/event notifications (option A). A network policy controls allowed IP ranges for account access, not repository origins (option B). An external access integration governs outbound network access from UDFs/procedures, not Git repository setup (option C).

citations.title: Setting up Snowflake to use Git

72:0.question: A data analyst runs a query with 'SAMPLE SYSTEM (10)' on a large table and separately runs the same query with 'SAMPLE BERNOULLI (10)'. Which statement correctly describes the difference between the SYSTEM (BLOCK) and BERNOULLI (ROW) sampling methods?
options: SYSTEM sampling evaluates each individual row independently, while BERNOULLI sampling selects entire micro-partitions
Both methods always return exactly the same number of rows for a given percentage
SYSTEM sampling selects rows based on micro-partitions (blocks) and is generally faster, while BERNOULLI sampling evaluates each row independently for a more random sample
BERNOULLI sampling can only be used with a fixed number of rows, while SYSTEM sampling can only be used with a percentage
72:0.explanation: SYSTEM (or BLOCK) sampling selects data at the micro-partition (block) level, which is faster but can yield a less uniform sample; BERNOULLI (or ROW) sampling evaluates each row independently based on the specified probability, producing a more truly random sample but typically taking longer. Neither method guarantees an exact row count for a percentage-based sample. The first option reverses the two methods' behavior, and BERNOULLI supports probability-based sampling.

75:0.question: An organization runs both a nightly heavy ETL batch process and interactive BI dashboard queries. The BI users complain that their queries slow down significantly during the ETL window because both workloads share a single virtual warehouse. Following Snowflake best practices, what is the recommended approach to prevent the ETL workload from impacting BI query performance?
options: Increase the size of the single shared warehouse so both workloads have more compute
Enable a resource monitor on the shared warehouse to throttle the ETL queries
Assign the ETL workload and the BI workload to separate, dedicated virtual warehouses
Convert the shared warehouse to a multi-cluster warehouse in maximized mode
75:0.explanation: Snowflake recommends separating distinct workloads onto separate, dedicated virtual warehouses so that each workload's compute is isolated and does not contend for resources. Because compute is decoupled, running ETL on its own warehouse ensures BI queries on a different warehouse are unaffected. Simply increasing size still shares resources between competing workloads. Resource monitors control credit consumption and can suspend warehouses, but do not isolate query performance between workloads. Multi-cluster warehouses address concurrency within a single workload but still run the same mixed workload together.

68:1.question: An engineer wants to create a materialized view but the CREATE statement fails. Which of the following are documented restrictions that could cause a materialized view definition to be rejected in Snowflake? (Select all that apply.)
options: The query includes an ORDER BY clause
The query joins two or more tables
The query includes a window function
The materialized view is defined on a standard permanent table
The query uses a HAVING clause
68:1.explanation: Snowflake documentation lists specific limitations for materialized view definitions: they cannot include an ORDER BY clause, cannot include joins (including self-joins) so they are limited to a single table, cannot use window functions, and cannot include HAVING clauses. Defining a materialized view on a standard permanent table is fully supported and is the normal use case, so that option is not a restriction.

74:0.question: A table named orders has a column details of type VARIANT that contains a JSON array of line items under the key "items". A developer wants to produce one output row for each element in the items array, along with the corresponding order's id column. Which query construct correctly achieves this?
options: SELECT o.id, f.value FROM orders o, TABLE(SPLIT(o.details:items)) f
SELECT o.id, f.value FROM orders o GROUP BY o.details:items
SELECT o.id, f.value FROM orders o, LATERAL FLATTEN(input => o.details:items) f
SELECT o.id, ARRAY_AGG(o.details:items) FROM orders o
74:0.explanation: LATERAL FLATTEN (a table function used with the LATERAL keyword or in the FROM clause) expands an input array or object into a set of rows, producing one row per array element. Referencing the outer table's column (o.details:items) as the input and joining laterally produces one output row per element per order. SPLIT operates on strings, not VARIANT arrays, and does not produce rows. GROUP BY aggregates rather than expands rows. ARRAY_AGG collapses values into a single array, the opposite of what is required.

75:1.question: A data engineering team is designing their compute strategy across a single Snowflake account. Which statements about using multiple virtual warehouses for workload isolation are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: Multiple warehouses can run concurrently, each with its own independent compute resources
Because compute and storage are separate, all warehouses access the same shared data without copying it
Each warehouse can be independently sized, started, and suspended according to its workload's needs
Two warehouses querying the same table will contend for that table's compute resources
Running each workload on its own warehouse prevents one workload from consuming compute needed by another
75:1.explanation: Virtual warehouses are independent compute clusters; multiple warehouses can operate concurrently, each sized, started, and suspended independently. Because Snowflake separates compute from storage, all warehouses access the same underlying data without duplication. Isolating workloads to separate warehouses prevents contention between them. The incorrect statement claims warehouses querying the same table contend for that table's compute — tables have no dedicated compute; each warehouse uses its own compute resources regardless of which table it queries.

77:0.question: A machine learning team is running Snowpark Python workloads that train models in memory and occasionally fail with out-of-memory errors on a standard warehouse. They want a warehouse type that provides more memory and compute resources per node to support these memory-intensive workloads. Which warehouse best fits this requirement?
options: A multi-cluster warehouse with a high maximum cluster count
A Snowpark-optimized warehouse
A warehouse with AUTO_SCALE scaling policy enabled
A serverless task-dedicated warehouse
77:0.explanation: Snowpark-optimized warehouses provide more memory (and larger compute resources) per node compared to standard warehouses, making them suitable for memory-intensive workloads such as ML training and large UDF/stored procedure execution in Snowpark. A multi-cluster warehouse adds clusters to handle concurrency, not per-node memory. A scaling policy controls how clusters start/stop for concurrency, not node memory. There is no distinct 'serverless task-dedicated warehouse' type that provides increased memory per node.

76:0.question: A single-cluster virtual warehouse is receiving more concurrent queries than it can process with its current compute resources. What happens to the queries that cannot be processed immediately?
options: The excess queries are automatically routed to the cloud services layer for execution
The queries are queued until running queries complete and resources become available
The queries fail immediately with a concurrency limit exceeded error
The warehouse automatically resizes to a larger size to run all queries at once
76:0.explanation: When a warehouse does not have enough resources (memory or compute) to process all submitted queries concurrently, the excess queries are queued and wait until running queries finish and resources free up. Queries are not routed to the cloud services layer for execution, and they do not fail immediately due to concurrency. A single-cluster warehouse does not auto-resize; automatic scaling of clusters requires a multi-cluster warehouse, which adds clusters rather than resizing a single warehouse.

74:1.question: When using the FLATTEN table function to query semi-structured data, which columns are available in the output for each flattened element? (Select all that apply.)
options: VALUE — the value of the element of the flattened array/object
INDEX — the index of the element if it is an array (NULL for objects)
PARTITION — the micro-partition that stores the element
KEY — the key if the flattened value is a map or object
PATH — the path to the element within the data structure
74:1.explanation: The FLATTEN function returns the columns SEQ, KEY, PATH, INDEX, VALUE, and THIS. VALUE holds the element's value, INDEX holds the array index (NULL for non-arrays), KEY holds the key for maps/objects (NULL for arrays), and PATH holds the path to the element. PARTITION is not a column returned by FLATTEN — micro-partition metadata is not exposed through FLATTEN output.

77:1.question: Which of the following statements about Snowpark-optimized warehouses are accurate? (Select all that apply.)
options: They are recommended for workloads that have large memory requirements, such as ML training use cases.
They are created by setting the warehouse type to SNOWPARK-OPTIMIZED.
They can only run Snowpark code and cannot execute standard SQL queries.
They provide more memory per node than standard warehouses of the same size.
They automatically eliminate all disk spilling regardless of workload size.
77:1.explanation: Snowpark-optimized warehouses are recommended for memory-intensive workloads such as ML training, are created by specifying the warehouse type SNOWPARK-OPTIMIZED, and provide more memory per node than standard warehouses. They are not restricted to Snowpark code — they can run standard SQL queries as well. They do not guarantee elimination of all disk spilling; sufficiently large workloads can still spill.

73:0.question: An analyst writes a query using 'ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY department ORDER BY salary DESC)' to rank employees within each department by salary. Two employees in the same department have identical salaries. How does ROW_NUMBER assign values to these two tied rows?
options: It assigns both rows the same number and skips the next number
It assigns both rows the same number without skipping the next number
It assigns each row a distinct, sequential number even though their ordering values are tied
It returns an error because ROW_NUMBER cannot be used when the ORDER BY column contains duplicate values
73:0.explanation: ROW_NUMBER assigns a unique, sequential integer to every row within a partition based on the ORDER BY, so tied rows still receive distinct numbers (the tie-break order among them is not guaranteed but the values are unique). Assigning the same number and skipping the next value describes RANK; assigning the same number without a gap describes DENSE_RANK. ROW_NUMBER does not error on duplicate ordering values.

76:1.question: A warehouse is running at its maximum concurrency, and additional queries submitted to it begin to queue rather than execute immediately. A data engineer wants to reduce query queuing on this single warehouse without changing its size. Which parameter directly controls how many concurrent queries a warehouse will attempt to run before subsequent queries are queued?
options: STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS
MAX_CONCURRENCY_LEVEL
AUTO_SUSPEND
MIN_CLUSTER_COUNT
76:1.explanation: MAX_CONCURRENCY_LEVEL sets the maximum number of concurrent statements a warehouse (or each cluster) will execute; when this level is reached, additional queries are queued. Lowering or raising it changes how queries queue on a single warehouse. STATEMENT_TIMEOUT_IN_SECONDS controls how long a statement can run before it is aborted, not concurrency. AUTO_SUSPEND controls idle suspension timing. MIN_CLUSTER_COUNT applies to multi-cluster warehouses and adds clusters rather than governing concurrency within a single running warehouse.

78:1.question: A table was created in a Snowflake Enterprise Edition account. The administrator did not explicitly set DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS on the table, database, or schema, and wants the maximum possible Time Travel retention for a permanent table on this edition. What is the maximum number of days of Time Travel retention that can be configured for a permanent table in Enterprise Edition?
options: 1 day
7 days
45 days
90 days
78:1.explanation: In Enterprise Edition (and higher), permanent objects can have DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS set to a maximum of 90 days. The default value is 1 day unless changed. Standard Edition is limited to a maximum of 1 day. 7 and 45 days are not documented maximums for Time Travel retention.

57:0.question: A data engineer runs 'ALTER GIT REPOSITORY my_repo FETCH;' after connecting Snowflake to a Git repository. What does this command accomplish?
options: It pushes local changes made in Snowflake back to the remote Git repository provider
It updates the Snowflake Git repository object with the latest changes (branches, tags, and files) from the remote repository
It permanently deletes the cached repository contents and disables further access
It converts the Git repository object into an external stage backed by cloud storage
57:0.explanation: The FETCH command retrieves the latest changes from the remote repository so the Snowflake Git repository object reflects the current branches, tags, and files. Snowflake Git integration is read-only from Snowflake's perspective, so it does not push changes back to the provider (making the first option wrong). FETCH does not delete cached contents or disable access, and it does not convert the object type; the Git repository object is created and managed as its own object type, not turned into an external stage.

citations.title: Using a Git repository in Snowflake

79:0.question: A data engineer accidentally dropped a permanent table two weeks ago. The database has a Time Travel retention period of 7 days, and that period has fully elapsed. The engineer needs to recover the data. Which statement correctly describes the recovery path at this point?
options: The engineer can restore the data using the UNDROP TABLE command since permanent tables are always recoverable via Time Travel
The data is permanently lost because Fail-safe only applies to transient tables, not permanent tables
The data can only be recovered by contacting Snowflake, because after Time Travel expires the data enters the 7-day Fail-safe period which is managed exclusively by Snowflake
The engineer can extend the Time Travel retention period retroactively to recover the data through self-service
79:0.explanation: After the Time Travel retention period ends, historical data for permanent tables moves into Fail-safe, which provides a (non-configurable) 7-day period during which the data may be recoverable only by Snowflake. It is not accessible via SQL commands like UNDROP or self-service. Option 0 is wrong because UNDROP only works within the Time Travel window. Option 1 is wrong because Fail-safe applies to permanent tables, not transient tables. Option 3 is wrong because Time Travel cannot be extended retroactively to recover already-expired data.

78:0.question: A developer accidentally ran an UPDATE that corrupted many rows in a permanent table. They want to view the table's contents exactly as they were 10 minutes ago to compare against the current data, without altering the current table. Assuming the table is within its Time Travel retention period, which approach lets them query the historical data?
options: Use the UNDROP TABLE command to view the prior state
Query the table using the AT or BEFORE clause with the OFFSET or TIMESTAMP option
Restore the table from Fail-safe using a SELECT statement
Set DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS to 0 and re-run the query
78:0.explanation: Time Travel allows querying historical data using the AT or BEFORE clause (with OFFSET, TIMESTAMP, or STATEMENT) in a SELECT statement to see a table as of a past point in time. UNDROP TABLE only recovers dropped objects, not a prior data state of an existing table. Fail-safe data is not accessible via SELECT and is recoverable only by Snowflake. Setting DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS to 0 would disable Time Travel and prevent historical queries.

80:0.question: An organization creates a transient table to hold intermediate ETL results. They want to understand the data protection characteristics of this table type. Which statement correctly describes Time Travel and Fail-safe behavior for transient tables?
options: Transient tables support up to 90 days of Time Travel and have a 7-day Fail-safe period
Transient tables support a maximum Time Travel retention of 1 day and have no Fail-safe period
Transient tables support no Time Travel and no Fail-safe period
Transient tables support up to 90 days of Time Travel but have no Fail-safe period
80:0.explanation: Transient tables can have a Time Travel retention period of either 0 or 1 day (maximum of 1 day), and they have no Fail-safe period, which helps reduce storage costs.
