Option 0 is wrong because 90-day Time Travel requires a permanent table on Enterprise Edition, and transient tables have no Fail-safe. Option 2 is wrong because transient tables can still have up to 1 day of Time Travel. Option 3 is wrong because transient tables are limited to 1 day of Time Travel, not 90.

citations.title: Working with temporary and transient tables

81:0.question: A developer clones a large 5 TB production table using CREATE TABLE ... CLONE to create a test copy. Which statement best describes what happens with respect to storage and data at the moment of cloning?
options: The clone physically duplicates all 5 TB of micro-partitions immediately, doubling storage usage
The clone shares the underlying micro-partitions with the source and only adds storage costs as data in either the source or clone is changed (copy-on-write)
The clone creates a live pointer so any change to the clone is immediately reflected in the source table
The clone can only be created if the source table has Time Travel disabled
81:0.explanation: Zero-copy cloning creates a copy based on metadata only; the clone initially references the same micro-partitions as the source, so no additional storage is consumed until the source or clone data is modified (copy-on-write). Option 0 is wrong because cloning does not physically duplicate data at creation. Option 2 is wrong because the clone is independent — changes to the clone do not affect the source and vice versa. Option 3 is wrong because Time Travel does not need to be disabled to clone a table.

82:0.question: A data provider uses Secure Data Sharing to grant a consumer account access to a table in one of the provider's databases. What is the fundamental characteristic of the data that the consumer receives access to through the share?
options: The data is copied into the consumer's account and periodically refreshed by a background task
The consumer receives a nightly snapshot of the shared table stored in their own account
No actual data is copied or transferred; the consumer accesses live, read-only data that resides in the provider's account
The data is replicated to the consumer's account only when both accounts are in the same region
82:0.explanation: With Secure Data Sharing, no actual data is copied or transferred between accounts. Sharing is accomplished through Snowflake's services layer and metadata store, so consumers access live, read-only data that continues to reside in the provider's account. Because there is no copying, the consumer's storage is not consumed by shared data, and any changes the provider makes are immediately visible. The 'copied' and 'snapshot' options describe replication or ETL, not sharing; sharing does not create a copy. The 'same region only' option is incorrect because same-region sharing works directly, and cross-region sharing is enabled via replication of the underlying database, but the defining characteristic of Secure Data Sharing itself is that no data is copied.

82:1.question: A company shares a database with a consumer account using Secure Data Sharing. The consumer wants to understand what compute resources are used and who pays for query processing on the shared data. Which statements are TRUE about how compute works for shared data? (Select all that apply.)
options: Consumers use their own virtual warehouses to query the shared data, and they pay for that compute
The provider's virtual warehouse automatically runs all queries that consumers submit against shared data
Shared data is read-only for consumers; they cannot add rows or update the provider's data through the share
Consumers are billed for storage of the shared data because a copy is placed in their account
A provider can add or remove objects from a share, and those changes affect consumer access to the data
82:1.explanation: In Secure Data Sharing, consumers query shared data using their own virtual warehouses, so consumers pay for the compute they use. Shared data is read-only to consumers — they cannot insert, update, or delete the provider's data. Providers can add or remove objects from a share, and those changes are reflected in what consumers can access. The provider's warehouse does NOT run consumer queries — that is incorrect. Consumers are not billed for storage of shared data because no copy is created in their account; the data remains in the provider's account.

79:1.question: A team is discussing how Snowflake performs Fail-safe data recovery after the Time Travel retention period has fully elapsed. Which statement correctly describes how Fail-safe recovery is initiated and its associated costs?
options: Users can query and recover Fail-safe data directly with SQL using AT/BEFORE clauses, incurring only compute costs
Fail-safe recovery is performed only by Snowflake and is not accessible to users through SQL; it uses Snowflake-managed serverless compute to which standard serverless compute billing applies
Fail-safe recovery is fully self-service via the UNDROP command and incurs no additional cost
Fail-safe data is retained indefinitely and recovery is free of both storage and compute charges
79:1.explanation: Per Snowflake documentation, Fail-safe provides a (non-configurable) period during which historical data may be recoverable by Snowflake only — it is not accessible to users and cannot be queried or recovered via SQL. Fail-safe data recovery uses Snowflake-managed serverless compute, and standard serverless compute billing applies (in addition to the storage costs for maintaining Fail-safe data). Option 0 is wrong because users cannot access Fail-safe data via SQL. Option 2 is wrong because UNDROP relies on Time Travel, not Fail-safe, and recovery is not self-service. Option 3 is wrong because Fail-safe is not indefinite and recovery incurs serverless compute costs.

73:1.question: An analyst needs to assign a sequential integer to each row within a partition, ordered by a column, such that tied rows still receive distinct consecutive numbers with no gaps. They are choosing between the RANK, DENSE_RANK, and ROW_NUMBER window functions. Which function assigns a unique, gapless sequential number to every row (including tied rows) within each partition?
options: RANK
ROW_NUMBER
DENSE_RANK
PERCENT_RANK
73:1.explanation: ROW_NUMBER returns a unique row number for each row within the window partition, starting at 1 and incrementing sequentially with no gaps, assigning distinct values even to tied rows. RANK assigns the same rank to ties and then skips subsequent numbers (produces gaps). DENSE_RANK assigns the same rank to ties but does NOT skip numbers, so tied rows share a value rather than receiving distinct consecutive numbers. PERCENT_RANK returns a relative rank as a fraction between 0 and 1, not a sequential integer.

83:0.question: An organization wants to replicate databases from one Snowflake account to another and enable client redirect for failover so applications can continue running if the primary account becomes unavailable. Which Snowflake edition is the MINIMUM required to use database and account failover/failback with client redirect?
options: Standard Edition
Enterprise Edition
Business Critical Edition
Virtual Private Snowflake (VPS) Edition
83:0.explanation: Failover and failback capabilities (including client redirect for business continuity and disaster recovery) require Business Critical Edition (or higher). Standard Edition supports basic replication of databases for read-only secondary copies, but failover/failback of databases and accounts requires Business Critical. Enterprise Edition is not sufficient for failover. VPS is higher than Business Critical and would technically qualify, but it is not the MINIMUM edition required — Business Critical is.

citations.title: Introduction to replication and failover across multiple accounts

72:1.question: A data engineer wants to make a sampled query deterministic (repeatable) by using the SEED keyword in the TABLESAMPLE / SAMPLE clause. According to Snowflake, with which sampling method(s) can the SEED (or REPEATABLE) parameter be used?
options: SEED can only be used with BERNOULLI (ROW) sampling
SEED can be used with both BERNOULLI (ROW) and SYSTEM (BLOCK) sampling equally
SEED can only be used with SYSTEM (BLOCK) sampling; using it with BERNOULLI (ROW) sampling produces an error
SEED is not supported by any sampling method in Snowflake
72:1.explanation: The Snowflake documentation for the SAMPLE / TABLESAMPLE construct states that the seed parameter (SEED / REPEATABLE) 'only applies to SYSTEM and BLOCK sampling.' Attempting to use SEED with ROW (BERNOULLI) sampling, e.g. SAMPLE ROW (10 ROWS) SEED(99), produces an error. Therefore SEED cannot be used to make BERNOULLI/ROW sampling deterministic. Option 0 is the reverse of the truth. Option 1 is wrong because the two methods are not treated equally with respect to SEED. Option 3 is wrong because SEED is supported for SYSTEM/BLOCK sampling.

83:1.question: A data engineer on Standard Edition wants to create a read-only secondary copy of a database in another Snowflake account in a different region for reporting purposes, but does NOT need automated failover for disaster recovery. Which statement best describes what is available to them?
options: Standard Edition cannot replicate databases at all; replication requires Enterprise Edition or higher
Standard Edition supports replicating databases to create read-only secondary databases, but failover/failback requires Business Critical Edition or higher
Standard Edition supports full account failover with client redirect but not read-only replication
Standard Edition supports replication only within the same region, never across regions
83:1.explanation: Standard Edition (and higher) supports database replication, which creates a read-only secondary database that can be refreshed from the primary. However, failover and failback (promoting a secondary to be the primary for business continuity) require Business Critical Edition or higher. Standard Edition does support cross-region and cross-cloud replication of databases, so the 'same region only' and 'cannot replicate at all' options are incorrect. The option claiming Standard supports failover with client redirect is incorrect because failover requires Business Critical.

citations.title: Introduction to replication and failover across multiple accounts

84:0.question: A provider wants to distribute their data and applications to consumers through the Snowflake Marketplace. Which statements accurately describe capabilities of the Snowflake Marketplace? (Select all that apply.)
options: Providers can publish listings that offer shared datasets to consumers without the consumer having to copy the data
Providers can offer Snowflake Native Apps through the Marketplace, allowing consumers to install applications that run in their own account
Marketplace listings can be either free or paid, allowing providers to monetize their data products
Consumers must move data out of Snowflake and into an external system before they can query a Marketplace listing
The Marketplace only supports sharing data within the same Snowflake region and cannot deliver listings across regions
84:0.explanation: The Snowflake Marketplace lets providers publish listings that share datasets with consumers using Secure Data Sharing, so consumers access the data without copying it. Providers can also offer Snowflake Native Apps that consumers install and run within their own account. Listings can be free or paid, enabling monetization of data products. Consumers do NOT need to move data out of Snowflake to query a listing — they query it directly in Snowflake. The Marketplace supports cross-region and cross-cloud delivery of listings (via auto-fulfillment/replication), so the 'same region only' claim is incorrect.

80:1.question: An administrator is comparing the Time Travel retention limits available for permanent tables across Snowflake editions. Which statement correctly describes the maximum Time Travel retention period configurable for permanent tables based on edition?
options: Standard Edition allows up to 90 days; Enterprise Edition allows up to 1 day
Both Standard and Enterprise Editions allow up to 90 days
Standard Edition allows a maximum of 1 day; Enterprise Edition (and higher) allows up to 90 days for permanent objects
All editions are limited to a maximum of 7 days of Time Travel retention
80:1.explanation: The Snowflake Time Travel documentation states that the retention period can be set from 0 up to 1 day for Standard Edition, and from 0 up to 90 days for permanent objects on Enterprise Edition (and higher). Option 0 reverses the values. Option 1 is wrong because Standard Edition is limited to 1 day. Option 3 confuses the Fail-safe period (7 days for permanent tables) with the Time Travel maximum.

85:0.question: A developer is building a Snowflake Native App to distribute through the Snowflake Marketplace. Which object is used to package the application's logic, setup script, and metadata so that it can be published as a listing for consumers to install?
options: An application package, which contains the app's data content, versions, and the manifest that defines the setup
A database share, which contains the compiled application binary
A resource monitor, which controls the app's compute consumption during installation
A secure UDF library, which is the required container for all Native App code
85:0.explanation: In the Snowflake Native App Framework, the provider creates an application package. The application package is the container that holds the app's data content, one or more versions, and references the manifest file and setup script that define the application. It is the object that is shared and published as a listing. A share alone does not package app logic and versions; resource monitors govern warehouse credit usage, not packaging; and while apps can contain UDFs, a UDF library is not the packaging container.

citations.title: About the Snowflake Native App Framework

85:1.question: When developing a Snowflake Native App, which file is REQUIRED and defines the application's properties, configuration, and setup script location that Snowflake uses when creating the application object?
options: The setup.sql file, which alone is sufficient to create the application without any other required files
The snowflake.yml project definition file, which is the only file Snowflake reads at install time
The manifest.yml file, which specifies the application's configuration and points to the setup script
The environment.yml file, which defines the app's Python dependencies and is mandatory for every app
85:1.explanation: The manifest file (manifest.yml) is a required file in the Snowflake Native App Framework. It defines the configuration and properties of the application and identifies the location of the setup script that runs during installation. The setup script is also required, but the manifest is what Snowflake reads to determine app configuration and locate that setup script — the setup script alone is not sufficient. snowflake.yml is a Snowflake CLI project definition used during development, not the app's required manifest read at install. environment.yml relates to Python/Snowpark dependencies and is not mandatory for every Native App.

citations.title: Add the manifest file to your application
About the Snowflake Native App Framework

84:1.question: A company wants to distribute an interactive application—not just a dataset—to consumers through the Snowflake Marketplace, so that consumers can install and run the application logic within their own Snowflake accounts using data and code the provider supplies. Which type of Snowflake offering enables a provider to package application logic (such as stored procedures and UDFs) along with data for consumers to install and run in their own account?
options: A secure view shared through a direct Secure Data Sharing outbound share
A Snowflake Native App built with the Snowflake Native App Framework
A materialized view published as a standard listing
A reader account provisioned for each consumer
84:1.explanation: The Snowflake Native App Framework lets providers build applications that bundle data content along with application logic (such as stored procedures and functions) and share them with consumers, who install and run the app within their own Snowflake account. This is distinct from sharing only data. A secure view via a direct share (option A) shares data but not runnable application logic. A materialized view as a standard listing (option C) still only shares data, not application code. A reader account (option D) is a mechanism for providers to give non-Snowflake customers access to shared data—it does not package or distribute an application to consumers to run in their own account.

citations.title: About the Snowflake Native App Framework

81:1.question: A data engineer needs to create a clone of a production database so a team can run experiments. They want to understand what is required to perform the clone. According to Snowflake documentation, which statement about zero-copy cloning is correct?
options: Cloning is only available in the Enterprise Edition or higher and cannot be performed in Standard Edition.
Cloning a table requires the source table to first be copied into an internal stage before the clone is created.

A clone is a writable, independent object that references the source object's existing micro-partitions at the time of cloning, and modifications to either the clone or the source do not affect the other.
Cloning a table automatically enables Time Travel on the clone for 90 days regardless of the source table's retention period.
81:1.explanation: Snowflake's zero-copy cloning creates a new, independent object that references the source object's existing storage (micro-partitions) at the moment of cloning. The clone is writable and independent — changes made to the clone or the source afterward do not affect the other object. Option A is wrong: cloning is available across editions and is not restricted to Enterprise. Option B is wrong: cloning is a metadata operation and does not require staging or physically copying data. Option D is wrong: cloning does not set a fixed 90-day Time Travel period; Time Travel retention is governed by the account/object DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS settings, not automatically extended by cloning.

0:0.question: In Snowflake's three-layer architecture, which layer is responsible for query optimization, authentication, metadata management, and access control?
options: The compute (query processing) layer
The cloud services layer
The centralized (database) storage layer
The virtual warehouse layer
0:0.explanation: The cloud services layer is the collection of services that coordinate activities across Snowflake, including authentication, infrastructure management, metadata management, query parsing and optimization, and access control. The compute layer (made up of virtual warehouses) executes queries but does not perform optimization or authentication. The storage layer holds data in compressed, columnar micro-partitions. 'Virtual warehouse layer' is simply another name for the compute layer, not a separate services layer.

1:1.question: Snowflake maintains metadata about the range of values for each column within each micro-partition. What is the primary performance benefit this metadata provides during query execution?
options: It allows Snowflake to automatically compress columns using a single optimal algorithm
It guarantees that queries never require a virtual warehouse to execute
It enables Snowflake to prune (skip) micro-partitions that cannot contain data matching query filters
It permits users to manually reorganize micro-partitions to improve clustering
1:1.explanation: Snowflake stores metadata about each micro-partition, including the range of values for each column. This enables query pruning: Snowflake can determine which micro-partitions are not relevant to a query's filter conditions and skip scanning them, improving performance. Compression is a separate benefit and Snowflake chooses algorithms per column, not a single algorithm driven by range metadata. Queries with compute still require a warehouse. Users do not manually reorganize micro-partitions—Snowflake manages this automatically.

5:0.question: A data engineer wants to define a view that references its own output to traverse a hierarchical employee-manager table, producing all reporting levels in a single query. Which type of view supports this self-referencing behavior in Snowflake?
options: Materialized view
Secure view
Recursive view
Standard view without a WITH clause
5:0.explanation: A recursive view is one that references itself in its own definition, which is exactly what is needed to traverse hierarchical (parent-child) data such as an employee-manager structure. Materialized views cannot be recursive and are limited to simple aggregations/projections on a single table. Secure views restrict exposure of the definition and underlying data but do not provide self-referencing traversal. A standard view without a recursive clause cannot reference itself.

4:0.question: A developer creates a temporary table in a session to hold intermediate results. What is the correct behavior of this temporary table with respect to its lifetime and Time Travel? (Select all that apply.)
options: The temporary table exists only for the duration of the session in which it was created and is dropped when the session ends
The temporary table is visible to all users across all sessions until it is explicitly dropped
The maximum Time Travel retention period for the temporary table is 1 day
A temporary table does not have a Fail-safe period
The temporary table persists across sessions but its data is purged after 24 hours
4:0.explanation: A temporary table exists only within the session in which it is created and persists only for the remainder of that session; it is not visible to other sessions or users (making option 1 and option 4 wrong). Temporary tables can have a Time Travel retention period of 0 or 1 day, but never more than 1 day. Like transient tables, temporary tables have no Fail-safe period, so no additional storage costs accrue after Time Travel ends.

4:1.question: A team needs a table type where Snowflake stores data in open Parquet files using the Apache Iceberg table format, allowing the data to be managed within Snowflake while remaining in an external cloud storage location the customer controls. Which table type meets this requirement?
options: External table
Transient table
Iceberg table
Dynamic table
4:1.explanation: Iceberg tables use the Apache Iceberg open table format and store data as Parquet files in customer-supplied cloud storage (external volume), while being usable as Snowflake tables. External tables reference data in external stages for read-only querying but do not use Iceberg format or provide full table management. Transient tables are internal Snowflake storage without Fail-safe. Dynamic tables materialize the results of a query and are refreshed automatically, not a format for storing Parquet in customer-controlled storage.

6:0.question: A Snowflake administrator notices that query result caching, authentication, and query parsing/optimization all happen without any active virtual warehouse being resumed. Which layer of Snowflake's architecture is responsible for these functions?
options: The virtual warehouse (compute) layer
The database storage layer
The cloud services layer
The client interface layer (Snowsight/SnowSQL)
6:0.explanation: The cloud services layer is the 'brain' of Snowflake and coordinates activities including authentication, infrastructure management, metadata management, query parsing and optimization, and access control. The virtual warehouse layer executes queries using compute resources but does not manage metadata or authentication. The storage layer holds the persisted data in compressed micro-partitions. Client interfaces are tools for connecting, not an architectural service layer.

2:0.question: A data engineer resizes a virtual warehouse from Small to Medium. Regarding warehouse sizing and credit consumption, which statement is correct?
options: Resizing from Small to Medium doubles the number of credits consumed per hour that the warehouse runs
Credit consumption is based only on the number of queries executed, not on warehouse size
A Medium warehouse consumes the same credits per hour as a Small warehouse but runs queries faster
Resizing to a larger warehouse automatically enables multi-cluster scaling
2:0.explanation: Each warehouse size consumes a set number of credits per full hour of runtime, and each size up the scale doubles the compute resources and the credit consumption rate. A Small consumes 2 credits/hour and a Medium consumes 4 credits/hour, so moving from Small to Medium doubles the per-hour credit rate. Credit consumption is based on warehouse size and running time (billed per second with a 60-second minimum), not solely on query count. A Medium does not cost the same as a Small. Resizing (scaling up) is distinct from multi-cluster (scaling out); enlarging a warehouse does not automatically enable multi-cluster mode.

5:1.question: Which statements accurately describe characteristics of materialized views in Snowflake? (Select all that apply.)
options: A materialized view stores its query results and is automatically maintained by Snowflake as the base table data changes
A materialized view can be defined only on a single table and cannot include joins
A materialized view can be based on the results of joining multiple tables together
Materialized views incur additional storage and background maintenance (compute) costs
Materialized views are available in all Snowflake editions including Standard Edition
5:1.explanation: Materialized views store precomputed results and are automatically maintained by Snowflake when base data changes, which is correct. They are restricted to a single table and cannot include joins, so the option stating they can join multiple tables is false. They do incur extra storage plus background compute costs for maintenance, which is correct. Materialized views require Enterprise Edition (or higher), not Standard Edition, so that option is false.

6:1.question: Which of the following operations are handled by the cloud services layer in Snowflake? (Select all that apply.)
options: Metadata management for objects such as tables and micro-partition statistics
Access control and authentication of users
Reading and writing the actual data files stored in cloud object storage
Query parsing and optimization
Executing the compute-intensive scan and join operations of a query
6:1.explanation: The cloud services layer coordinates authentication, infrastructure management, metadata management, query parsing/optimization, and access control—so metadata management, access control/authentication, and query parsing/optimization are all correct. Reading and writing actual data files is a function tied to the storage layer, and executing compute-intensive scan/join processing is performed by virtual warehouses in the compute layer, so those two options are incorrect.

8:0.question: A Snowflake account administrator is reviewing charges for the Cloud Services layer. Under what condition are Cloud Services charges billed to the account?
options: Cloud Services usage is always billed in full regardless of virtual warehouse usage
Cloud Services usage is billed only when it exceeds 10% of the daily compute credits used by virtual warehouses
Cloud Services is never billed because it is included free with all editions
Cloud Services usage is billed only when it exceeds 20% of the monthly storage cost
8:0.explanation: Snowflake bills for Cloud Services only when the daily Cloud Services consumption exceeds 10% of the daily compute credits consumed by virtual warehouses. The 10% adjustment is calculated daily. Cloud Services is not always billed in full, nor is it entirely free — usage below the 10% threshold is not charged. Storage cost is unrelated to the Cloud Services billing threshold.

7:0.question: A data scientist is running memory-intensive machine learning training workloads in Snowpark that repeatedly spill to remote storage on a standard virtual warehouse. Which type of warehouse is specifically designed to provide additional memory and compute resources per node to better support such workloads?
options: A multi-cluster standard warehouse scaled to maximum clusters
A Snowpark-optimized warehouse
A standard warehouse set to the smallest size to reduce spilling
A resource-monitored warehouse with auto-suspend disabled
7:0.explanation: Snowpark-optimized warehouses provide more memory (and, depending on configuration, more compute) per node than standard warehouses, making them recommended for memory-intensive workloads such as ML training and certain UDF/stored procedure executions. Scaling a multi-cluster standard warehouse adds concurrency (more clusters for more queries) but does not increase per-node memory. Using the smallest standard size would reduce, not increase, available memory. Disabling auto-suspend affects cost/idle behavior, not memory capacity.

7:1.question: A data engineering team is running large-scale machine learning training jobs in a stored procedure that repeatedly fails with memory-related errors on a standard warehouse, even after increasing the warehouse size. Which type of warehouse is specifically designed to provide more memory and compute resources per node to address this workload?
options: A multi-cluster warehouse configured in maximized mode
A Snowpark-optimized warehouse
A standard warehouse with the SCALING_POLICY set to ECONOMY
A serverless task warehouse
7:1.explanation: Snowpark-optimized warehouses provide 16x the memory per node (and larger cache) compared to standard warehouses, making them recommended for memory-intensive workloads such as ML training and large UDF/UDTF/stored procedure execution. A multi-cluster warehouse in maximized mode only adds more clusters of the same node type to handle concurrency — it does not add memory per node. Scaling policy (ECONOMY/STANDARD) only governs how clusters start/stop in a multi-cluster warehouse and does not change per-node memory. 'Serverless task warehouse' is not a warehouse type you provision to solve per-node memory; serverless compute is managed by Snowflake.

9:0.question: A developer wants to run ad hoc SQL statements from the command line and also automate scripts for CI/CD pipelines. Which Snowflake command-line client is a traditional, dedicated SQL client for executing SQL statements interactively or in batch mode?
options: Snowflake CLI (snow)
SnowSQL
Snowsight worksheets
The Snowflake VS Code extension
9:0.explanation: SnowSQL is Snowflake's traditional command-line client, built for connecting to Snowflake and executing SQL queries interactively or in batch/scripted mode. The newer Snowflake CLI (snow) is oriented toward developing and managing apps, Snowpark, Streamlit, and Native Apps rather than being a dedicated SQL client. Snowsight is the web-based UI (worksheets), not a command-line tool. The VS Code extension is an IDE extension, not a command-line client.

8:1.question: Which of the following operations are handled by the Cloud Services layer and could therefore contribute to Cloud Services credit consumption? Select all that apply.
options: Authentication and access control
Query parsing and optimization
Reading and scanning micro-partition data from storage to satisfy a SELECT
Infrastructure and metadata management
Executing a JOIN across two large tables in a virtual warehouse
8:1.explanation: The Cloud Services layer coordinates activities such as authentication, access control, query parsing/optimization, infrastructure management, and metadata management. Scanning micro-partition data and executing JOINs are compute operations performed by virtual warehouses, not the Cloud Services layer.

3:1.question: A data team wants to use materialized views, extend the maximum Time Travel retention period beyond 1 day up to 90 days, and use multi-cluster warehouses. What is the minimum Snowflake edition that unlocks ALL of these capabilities?
options: Enterprise Edition
Standard Edition
Business Critical Edition
Virtual Private Snowflake (VPS)
3:1.explanation: Enterprise Edition is the minimum edition that unlocks materialized views, extended Time Travel (up to 90 days versus a maximum of 1 day in Standard), and multi-cluster warehouses. Standard Edition supports only up to 1 day of Time Travel and does not offer materialized views or multi-cluster warehouses. Business Critical and VPS include these features but are higher (not minimum) editions.

2:1.question: A virtual warehouse is configured as a multi-cluster warehouse in auto-scale mode with a minimum of 1 cluster and a maximum of 4 clusters. Each cluster is size Medium. During a peak period, all 4 clusters run for a full hour. How does Snowflake bill credit consumption for this warehouse during that hour?
options: Credits are billed only for the minimum cluster count (1 Medium cluster) regardless of how many clusters run
Credits are billed at a single Medium rate plus a fixed 10% surcharge per additional cluster

Credits are billed as the per-second/per-hour rate of one Medium cluster multiplied by the number of running clusters, so 4 Medium clusters running a full hour consume 4× the credits of one Medium cluster
Credits are billed based on the largest single warehouse size Snowflake could have used instead of scaling out
2:1.explanation: In a multi-cluster warehouse, each running cluster consumes credits at the rate of its warehouse size, and total consumption is the sum across all running clusters. A Medium warehouse consumes 4 credits/hour; four Medium clusters running for one hour consume 16 credits (4× one cluster). Billing is per-second with a 60-second minimum each time a cluster starts. Option 0 is wrong because auto-scale can start additional clusters that are billed. Option 1 invents a nonexistent surcharge. Option 3 misdescribes scaling out as if it were scaling up.

9:1.question: A team is standardizing on client tools for interacting with Snowflake. Which of the following statements correctly describes a Snowflake client interface? Select all that apply.
options: Snowsight is the web-based interface used to write queries, build dashboards, and manage the account
SnowSQL is installed as a standalone executable that must be downloaded and installed on the client machine
The Snowflake CLI (snow) is designed to support developing and managing applications such as Snowpark, Streamlit, and Native Apps
Snowsight requires a separate desktop installation before it can be used
9:1.explanation: Snowsight is Snowflake's web-based UI for querying, dashboards, and account management, and it is accessed through a browser with no desktop installation required — so the last option is false. SnowSQL is a standalone client that is downloaded and installed on the client machine. The Snowflake CLI (snow) is a newer command-line tool designed for developing and managing applications including Snowpark, Streamlit, and Snowflake Native Apps.

0:1.question: A data engineer notices that Snowflake automatically handles infrastructure management, transaction coordination, and query parsing without requiring any user-provisioned resources for these tasks. Which layer of Snowflake's three-layer architecture provides these management services?
options: The virtual warehouse (compute) layer
The database storage layer
The cloud services layer
The local disk cache layer
0:1.explanation: The cloud services layer is the collection of services that coordinate activities across Snowflake, tying together all components to process user requests. Per Snowflake documentation, it handles authentication, infrastructure management, metadata management, query parsing and optimization, and access control. The compute (virtual warehouse) layer executes queries using MPP compute clusters. The storage layer holds the data in Snowflake's optimized columnar format. 'Local disk cache' is a component within a virtual warehouse, not a distinct architectural layer.

citations.title: Overview of the Data Cloud (Snowflake Architecture)

13:0.question: A data scientist is using Snowflake Notebooks to explore data. Which statement correctly describes a capability of Snowflake Notebooks?
options: Notebooks support only SQL cells and cannot execute Python code
Notebooks are limited to Markdown documentation and cannot run any executable code
Notebooks allow cells containing SQL, Python, and Markdown to be combined within a single notebook
Notebooks can run Python but require an external Jupyter server outside of Snowflake to execute cells
13:0.explanation: Snowflake Notebooks provide an interactive, cell-based development interface where you can mix SQL cells, Python cells, and Markdown cells within the same notebook. Option A is wrong because Python is supported. Option B is wrong because notebooks execute code, not just render Markdown. Option D is wrong because notebook cells run on Snowflake's compute (a virtual warehouse or container runtime), not on an external Jupyter server.

11:1.question: An organization is choosing where their Snowpark code executes to minimize data movement out of Snowflake. Which statement accurately describes where Snowpark client-side code and Snowpark server-side objects (such as UDFs and stored procedures) run?
options: All Snowpark code, including the client library, executes exclusively inside Snowflake's cloud services layer
Snowpark UDFs and stored procedures must be written in SQL because Python cannot run inside Snowflake
The Snowpark DataFrame client library runs where the application runs, while Snowpark UDFs and stored procedures execute on Snowflake's virtual warehouse compute
Snowpark code can only run on the client and never executes within Snowflake compute
11:1.explanation: The Snowpark library provides an API that runs in the client application; the DataFrame operations it builds are pushed down to execute on Snowflake's compute. Additionally, Snowpark supports writing UDFs, UDTFs, and stored procedures in Python, Java, or Scala that execute directly within Snowflake's virtual warehouse compute. Option A is wrong because processing occurs on virtual warehouses, not the cloud services layer. Option B is wrong because Python (and Java/Scala) code can indeed execute inside Snowflake. Option D is wrong because Snowpark specifically enables server-side execution.

12:1.question: A team lead wants to share a Streamlit in Snowflake app with other users in the account so they can run it. Which mechanism controls whether another user is able to view and run a Streamlit in Snowflake app?
options: Streamlit apps bypass RBAC entirely and are visible to every user in the account by default
Access is granted only through Secure Data Sharing shares, not through roles
A user must be granted the CREATE STREAMLIT privilege to view any existing app
Role-based access control (RBAC) privileges on the Streamlit object, granted to a role that the user has, determine who can view and run the app
Access is limited exclusively to the ACCOUNTADMIN role and cannot be delegated
12:1.explanation: A Streamlit in Snowflake app is a schema-level object secured by RBAC. To share an app, the owner grants the appropriate privileges (such as USAGE) on the Streamlit object to a role, and users with that role can view and run the app. Option A is wrong because apps are not universally visible. Option B is wrong because sharing within an account is done via role grants, not required to use a data share. Option C is wrong because CREATE STREAMLIT allows creating apps, not viewing an existing one. Option E is wrong because access is not restricted to ACCOUNTADMIN and can be delegated via roles.

3:0.question: A company must satisfy a regulatory requirement to use a customer-managed encryption key combined with a Snowflake-managed key (Tri-Secret Secure), and it also needs support for compliance offerings such as HIPAA and PCI DSS. What is the minimum Snowflake edition that provides these capabilities?
options: Standard Edition
Enterprise Edition
Business Critical Edition
Virtual Private Snowflake (VPS)
3:0.explanation: Per Snowflake's edition documentation, Business Critical Edition (formerly Enterprise for Sensitive Data) adds enhanced security and data protection for organizations with sensitive data, including support for Tri-Secret Secure using a customer-managed key, and support for regulatory compliance such as HIPAA and PCI DSS. Standard and Enterprise editions do not include Tri-Secret Secure. Virtual Private Snowflake (VPS) provides an even higher level of isolation but is not the minimum edition that satisfies these requirements — Business Critical is.

13:1.question: A data scientist is working in a Snowflake Notebook and wants to reference the result of a SQL cell inside a subsequent Python cell to continue their analysis. Which capability of Snowflake Notebooks makes this possible?
options: Notebooks require all cells to use the same language, so the SQL result must first be exported to a stage before Python can read it.
A SQL cell's results can be accessed in a later Python cell as a Snowpark DataFrame or a pandas DataFrame by referencing the SQL cell's name.
Python cells can only reference other Python cells; cross-language referencing between SQL and Python is not supported.
The SQL result is automatically written to a permanent table that the Python cell must query with a new SQL statement.
13:1.explanation: Snowflake Notebooks support cells in SQL, Python, and Markdown within the same notebook. The output of a SQL cell can be referenced in a subsequent Python cell using the SQL cell's name, and it is available as either a Snowpark DataFrame or a pandas DataFrame. Option A is wrong because notebooks explicitly support multiple languages in one notebook without staging. Option C is wrong because cross-language referencing is a supported feature. Option D is wrong because no permanent table is automatically created — the result is passed directly as a DataFrame.

citations.title: Reference cells and variables in Snowflake Notebooks

14:1.question: An analyst needs to classify each row of a customer feedback table into one of several predefined categories such as 'complaint', 'praise', or 'question', without training a model. Which Snowflake Cortex function is purpose-built to assign text to one of a set of user-provided categories?
options: SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX.CLASSIFY_TEXT
SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX.EXTRACT_ANSWER
SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX.EMBED_TEXT_768
SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX.SENTIMENT
14:1.explanation: SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX.CLASSIFY_TEXT (also available as AI_CLASSIFY) classifies free-form text into one of a set of categories that you provide, without requiring model training. EXTRACT_ANSWER pulls an answer to a question from a source document, EMBED_TEXT_768 generates a vector embedding of text, and SENTIMENT returns only a numeric sentiment score rather than assigning a custom category label.

1:0.question: A database administrator is reviewing how Snowflake physically organizes table data on disk. Which statements accurately describe Snowflake micro-partitions? (Select all that apply)
options: Each micro-partition contains between 50 MB and 500 MB of uncompressed data
Data within a micro-partition is stored in a columnar format
Micro-partitions are created automatically as data is loaded or inserted into a table
Users must manually define micro-partition boundaries when creating a table
Snowflake stores metadata about each micro-partition, including the range of values for each column
1:0.explanation: Per Snowflake documentation, each micro-partition contains between 50 MB and 500 MB of uncompressed data (the actual stored size is smaller because data is always compressed), data is organized in a columnar fashion within each micro-partition, and micro-partitions are created automatically as rows are added — no user action is required. Snowflake also stores metadata about the range of values for each column in each micro-partition. The claim that users manually define micro-partition boundaries is false; micro-partitioning is automatic and transparent.

11:0.question: A data engineer wants to use the Snowpark API to build a query that transforms data in a Snowflake table. The code is written in Python and constructs a DataFrame with filter and aggregation operations. When does the actual computation get pushed down to run on Snowflake's virtual warehouse compute?
options: Immediately when each transformation method (such as filter or group_by) is called on the DataFrame
When an action such as collect(), show(), or count() is called, triggering execution of the lazily-built query
Only when the Python code is deployed as a stored procedure and scheduled by a task
Continuously in the background as the DataFrame object is held in the client session
11:0.explanation: Snowpark DataFrames use lazy evaluation. Transformation methods like filter() and group_by() build up the query plan but do not execute anything. The SQL is generated and pushed down to the Snowflake virtual warehouse only when an action (evaluation) method such as collect(), show(), count(), or save is invoked. This is why option A is wrong (transformations are lazy), option C is wrong (execution does not require a stored procedure or task), and option D is wrong (there is no continuous background execution).

citations.title: Working with DataFrames in Snowpark Python

15:0.question: A team is building a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) chatbot and chooses Cortex Search to retrieve relevant document chunks. Which statement best describes what makes Cortex Search a 'hybrid' search service?
options: It runs partly in Snowflake and partly on the customer's on-premises infrastructure.
It supports both structured relational tables and unstructured file stages at query time.
It combines semantic (vector) search with keyword (lexical) search to improve retrieval quality.
It can operate against both Snowflake-managed tables and external Iceberg tables simultaneously.
15:0.explanation: Cortex Search is described as a hybrid search service because it combines vector-based (semantic) search with keyword-based (lexical) search, along with reranking, to deliver high-quality retrieval results — which is especially useful for RAG applications. The other options misinterpret 'hybrid' as referring to hybrid deployment infrastructure or a mix of data source types, which is not what the term refers to for Cortex Search.

15:1.question: A developer is creating a Cortex Search Service over a table of support articles. Which requirement must be satisfied when defining the search service?
options: The service must be backed by a materialized view rather than a base table.
A search column containing the text to be indexed must be specified, and it must be a text (string) column.
The source data must first be manually converted into vector embeddings before creating the service.
A dedicated multi-cluster warehouse must be assigned exclusively to the search service and cannot be shared.
15:1.explanation: When creating a Cortex Search Service, you must specify a search column that holds the text to be indexed, and this column must be of a text/string type. Cortex Search automatically generates the embeddings and manages indexing internally, so option C is incorrect — you do not manually create embeddings. The source can be a table or view (not specifically a materialized view), making option A wrong. A warehouse is used to materialize and refresh the underlying query, but it need not be an exclusive dedicated multi-cluster warehouse, so option D is wrong.

14:0.question: A developer wants to use a Cortex AISQL function to answer a free-form natural-language question about a body of text stored in a column, providing both the prompt and the source text. Which Cortex function is designed for this general-purpose text generation and question-answering task?
options: SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX.SENTIMENT
SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX.SUMMARIZE
SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX.COMPLETE
SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX.TRANSLATE
14:0.explanation: SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX.COMPLETE (also available as AI_COMPLETE) generates a response to a given prompt using a selected large language model, making it the general-purpose function for free-form text generation and question answering. SENTIMENT returns a sentiment score for text, SUMMARIZE produces a summary of text, and TRANSLATE converts text between languages — none of these accept an arbitrary user prompt for general question answering.

17:0.question: A data engineer needs to predict future daily order volumes based on several years of historical sales data stored in a Snowflake table. Which Snowflake ML capability is purpose-built for producing these time-series predictions using a simple SQL interface?
options: The ANOMALY_DETECTION function class
The CLASSIFICATION function class
The FORECASTING function class
The TOP_INSIGHTS function class
17:0.explanation: The FORECASTING ML function (part of Snowflake's ML-powered functions) is designed to generate time-series forecasts, such as predicting future values based on historical data, using SQL. ANOMALY_DETECTION (wrong) flags outliers in time-series data rather than forecasting future values. CLASSIFICATION (wrong) predicts discrete categorical labels. TOP_INSIGHTS (wrong, formerly Contribution Explorer) surfaces the dimensions most responsible for a metric's change, not forward-looking predictions.

16:1.question: Which of the following statements about Cortex Analyst are accurate? (Select all that apply.)
options: Cortex Analyst is exposed as a REST API endpoint that applications can call to submit natural-language questions
The semantic model used by Cortex Analyst can be stored as a YAML file on a Snowflake stage or referenced as a semantic view
Cortex Analyst automatically bypasses row access policies and masking policies to return complete underlying data
Cortex Analyst executes the generated SQL using the privileges and role of the user or application calling it, so existing access controls still apply
16:1.explanation: Cortex Analyst is accessed through a REST API, allowing integration into applications and chat interfaces (correct). Its semantic model can be provided as a YAML file staged in Snowflake or referenced via a semantic view (correct). Because the generated SQL runs within the caller's session, existing role-based access controls, row access policies, and masking policies remain in force (correct). The claim that Cortex Analyst bypasses row access or masking policies is false — governance controls are always enforced on the underlying data.

17:1.question: A machine learning team wants to store, version, and manage trained models (including scikit-learn and PyTorch models) so they can be discovered and invoked directly within Snowflake. Which Snowflake capability provides this centralized model management?
options: The Snowflake Model Registry
The ANOMALY_DETECTION function class
A Snowflake internal named stage configured with a directory table
The Feature Store's entity catalog
17:1.explanation: The Snowflake Model Registry is the capability that lets teams securely store, version, and manage ML models (including those built with popular frameworks such as scikit-learn, XGBoost, and PyTorch) and invoke them within Snowflake. ANOMALY_DETECTION (wrong) is a built-in ML function for finding outliers, not a model management system. An internal stage with a directory table (wrong) stores files but does not provide model versioning, metadata, or inference methods. The Feature Store (wrong) manages ML features, not the trained models themselves.

16:0.question: A business analyst wants to query sales data using plain English questions like 'What were total revenues by region last quarter?' without writing SQL. Which Snowflake feature is designed to provide a conversational, natural-language interface that generates SQL against structured data, and what artifact must be provided to it for reliable results?
options: Cortex Search, which requires a search service definition over the tables
Cortex Analyst, which requires a semantic model file (YAML) describing the tables, columns, and relationships
Document AI, which requires a trained extraction model over the tables
Cortex Complete, which requires a fine-tuned LLM trained on the sales data
16:0.explanation: Cortex Analyst is the Snowflake feature that lets users ask questions in natural language and get generated SQL and answers over structured data. To produce reliable results, Cortex Analyst relies on a semantic model (a YAML specification) that describes the tables, columns, business terms, and relationships. Cortex Search (wrong) is for retrieval/RAG over text documents. Document AI (wrong) extracts data from unstructured documents. Cortex Complete (wrong) is a general LLM text-generation function and does not use a semantic model to generate SQL over your structured data.

12:0.question: A developer is building an interactive data application with Streamlit in Snowflake (SiS) and is deciding how the app's Python code should be executed. According to Snowflake, what compute options can be used to run the Python code that backs a Streamlit in Snowflake app?
options: Only a serverless task can run the Python code, because Streamlit apps are event-driven.
Only the cloud services layer runs the Python, so no compute needs to be selected.
You can choose between running the app on a virtual warehouse or on a container runtime.
The Python must run on the user's local machine while only SQL executes in Snowflake.
12:0.explanation: Snowflake documentation states that for Streamlit in Snowflake you can choose between a warehouse and a container runtime to run the app. Option 0 is incorrect because tasks are not the compute mechanism for SiS apps. Option 1 is incorrect because the Python execution is not handled solely by the cloud services layer. Option 3 is incorrect because Streamlit in Snowflake runs entirely within Snowflake, not on a user's local machine.

20:0.question: A team wants to store latitude/longitude points and polygons in Snowflake and perform spatial calculations such as distance and containment. Which Snowflake data type is designed to represent features in a planar (flat) coordinate system as opposed to a spherical model of the Earth?
options: GEOGRAPHY
GEOMETRY
VARIANT
VECTOR
20:0.explanation: GEOMETRY represents features in a planar (Euclidean, Cartesian) coordinate system. GEOGRAPHY models features on a spheroidal representation of the Earth's surface. VARIANT is a semi-structured type and VECTOR is used for storing embeddings, neither of which is a spatial coordinate-system type.

10:0.question: A data engineering team is choosing between a Snowflake-managed Iceberg table and an externally managed Iceberg table that uses a remote Iceberg REST catalog. They want the configuration that provides full Snowflake platform support, including the ability to run both DML and DDL, plus features like time travel and native performance optimizations. Which statement accurately describes the capabilities they can expect?
options: Externally managed Iceberg tables that use a remote Iceberg REST catalog are strictly read-only from Snowflake, so DML is impossible regardless of catalog type.
A Snowflake-managed Iceberg table (Snowflake as the catalog) provides full read and write access along with the broadest set of Snowflake platform features.
Both Snowflake-managed and externally managed Iceberg tables offer identical feature parity, including full platform support for all Snowflake features.
Only Iceberg tables stored in Snowflake's internal storage (not an external volume) can support DML operations.
10:0.explanation: Snowflake-managed Iceberg tables, where Snowflake serves as the catalog, provide full read and write access and the most complete set of Snowflake platform features. Option 0 is incorrect: Snowflake does support writes to externally managed tables that use a remote Iceberg REST catalog, so those tables are not strictly read-only. Option 2 is incorrect because externally managed tables do not have full feature parity with Snowflake-managed tables — Snowflake-managed tables have the broadest support. Option 3 is incorrect because Snowflake-managed Iceberg tables can use an external volume for storage and still support DML; the storage location does not determine DML capability.

19:0.question: A data engineer needs to store a column that can hold values of many different underlying types (numbers, strings, arrays, and objects) in a single Snowflake column, because the incoming JSON documents have inconsistent structures. Which semi-structured data type is designed specifically to store a value of any other Snowflake data type, including OBJECT and ARRAY?
options: OBJECT
ARRAY
VARIANT
TEXT
19:0.explanation: VARIANT is a universal semi-structured type that can store a value of any other type, including OBJECT and ARRAY. OBJECT stores collections of key-value pairs and ARRAY stores ordered lists, so neither can universally represent every type. TEXT (VARCHAR) is a structured character type and is not a semi-structured type.

18:0.question: A team is reviewing how automatic maintenance operations are billed in Snowflake. They want to understand which serverless compute feature is responsible for automatically reclustering data in a table that has a defined clustering key, without requiring the team to run or manage a virtual warehouse for that maintenance. Which Snowflake feature performs this?
options: Search Optimization Service, which handles all background reclustering of clustered tables.
Query Acceleration Service, which offloads reclustering to serverless compute.
Automatic Clustering, which uses Snowflake-managed serverless compute to maintain the clustering of a clustered table.
Multi-cluster warehouses, which automatically recluster tables when additional clusters spin up.
18:0.explanation: Automatic Clustering is the Snowflake serverless feature that continuously and automatically maintains the clustering of a table with a clustering key, using Snowflake-managed compute resources that are billed based on consumption. Option 0 is incorrect because Search Optimization Service improves point-lookup query performance and does not perform reclustering. Option 1 is incorrect because Query Acceleration Service offloads portions of query execution, not clustering maintenance. Option 3 is incorrect because multi-cluster warehouses add clusters to handle query concurrency and do not recluster table data.

18:1.question: An administrator wants to identify how Snowflake bills for serverless features. For serverless features, how is compute cost determined, in contrast to a user-managed virtual warehouse?
options: Serverless features are billed based on the compute resources used while the operation runs, using Snowflake-managed compute rather than a user-provisioned warehouse.
Serverless features are always free of charge and consume no credits.
Serverless features are billed at a flat monthly fee regardless of usage.
Serverless features can only be billed by attaching them to a user-managed virtual warehouse that must remain running.
18:1.explanation: Serverless features use Snowflake-managed compute resources that automatically scale, and cost is based on the total usage of those resources measured in credits while the operation runs — you do not provision or manage a warehouse for them. Option 1 is incorrect because serverless features do consume credits. Option 2 is incorrect because billing is usage-based, not a flat monthly fee. Option 3 is incorrect because serverless features specifically do not require a user-managed warehouse to run.

21:0.question: An architect is designing a table that uses a sequence to auto-generate unique numeric identifiers for new rows. Which statement accurately describes how Snowflake sequences behave?
options: Sequences guarantee that generated values are gap-free (contiguous) with no skipped numbers.
Sequences guarantee that generated values are unique but do not guarantee that there are no gaps.
Sequences reset to their start value automatically at the beginning of each day.
A sequence can only be referenced in the DEFAULT clause of a single table's column.
Sequences produce values only in strictly ascending order regardless of the specified increment.
21:0.explanation: Snowflake sequences are used to generate unique numbers, but the values are not guaranteed to be gap-free (contiguous) — gaps can occur. Sequences do not reset daily. A sequence can be shared and referenced by more than one table. The increment can be positive or negative, so values are not necessarily strictly ascending. Only the statement about uniqueness without contiguity guarantee is correct.

10:1.question: A data architect is planning storage for a Snowflake-managed Iceberg table. They want to understand the storage options that Snowflake supports for this table type. Which of the following are valid storage configurations for a Snowflake-managed Iceberg table? (Select all that apply.)
options: Storing the table's data and metadata files in your own external cloud storage that is referenced through an external volume.
Storing the table's data and metadata files in Snowflake-managed storage, where Snowflake stores and manages the Iceberg files for you.
Storing the table's data in an external Iceberg REST catalog owned by a third-party provider such as AWS Glue.
Storing the table's data only in a Snowflake internal named stage created with the TYPE = ICEBERG option.
10:1.explanation: For a Snowflake-managed Iceberg table, you can choose EITHER to store the table files in your own external cloud storage via an external volume, OR to use Snowflake storage, where Snowflake stores and manages the Iceberg table files for you. Option 2 describes an externally managed table using an external catalog, not a Snowflake-managed table. Option 3 is incorrect because Snowflake-managed Iceberg tables do not use internal named stages with a TYPE = ICEBERG option for their storage — that is not a documented mechanism.

22:1.question: Match the behavior to the correct URL type for staged unstructured files: 'A permanent URL that requires the user to authenticate to Snowflake and be authorized before the file is served.' Which URL type is being described?
options: Pre-signed URL
Scoped URL
File URL
Directory URL
22:1.explanation: Correct: A file URL is a permanent URL that identifies the database, schema, stage, and relative path to the file; accessing the file requires the user to authenticate and have appropriate privileges. Incorrect: A pre-signed URL is a simple HTTPS URL that is already authenticated within the URL, so users do not need to authenticate separately. A scoped URL is an encoded, temporary URL that expires. 'Directory URL' is not one of Snowflake's defined URL types for accessing staged files.

24:0.question: A data engineer wants to run SQL queries against Parquet files stored in an Amazon S3 bucket without first loading the data into Snowflake tables. Which Snowflake object is designed for this purpose?
options: A materialized view built on the staged files
A Snowpipe object pointing to the S3 bucket
An external table defined over an external stage referencing the S3 location
A transient table populated by COPY INTO
24:0.explanation: An external table lets you query data stored in an external stage (such as an S3 location) as if the data were inside a table, without loading it into Snowflake. Snowpipe (option B) loads data into a Snowflake table rather than querying it in place. A materialized view (option A) cannot be built directly on raw staged files without an underlying external table, and a transient table loaded via COPY INTO (option D) requires actually loading the data, which is what the engineer wants to avoid.

23:0.question: An application team is building a Java-based service that must connect to Snowflake and issue SQL queries. Which Snowflake connectivity option is specifically designed for use with Java applications?
options: The Python Connector, which uses a Java Virtual Machine wrapper
The ODBC driver, which is the only supported option for compiled languages
The Node.js driver, which is optimized for JVM-based frameworks
The JDBC driver, which provides a standard Java API for connecting to Snowflake
23:0.explanation: Correct: Snowflake provides a JDBC driver that developers can use to connect Java applications to Snowflake using the standard JDBC API. Incorrect: The Python Connector is for Python applications, not Java. The ODBC driver is for applications built on the ODBC standard (commonly C/C++ and BI tools), and it is not the only option for compiled languages. The Node.js driver is for JavaScript/Node.js applications, not JVM frameworks.

25:1.question: A developer created a stage but finds that querying its directory table returns no rows even though files exist in the stage. What is the most likely explanation and required action?
options: Directory tables require Enterprise Edition, so the account must be upgraded first
Directory tables are only supported on external stages, so an internal stage must be recreated as external
The directory table metadata is not automatically populated and must be refreshed, for example with ALTER STAGE ... REFRESH or by enabling automatic refresh
Directory tables must be created as a separate CREATE DIRECTORY TABLE object that references the stage
25:1.explanation: A directory table's metadata is not always kept current automatically; when files are added the metadata must be refreshed, either manually with ALTER STAGE <name> REFRESH or by configuring automatic refresh via event notifications. There is no separate CREATE DIRECTORY TABLE statement—the directory table is enabled through the DIRECTORY parameter on the stage (option D is wrong). Directory tables are supported on both internal and external stages (option B is wrong), and they are not restricted to a particular Snowflake edition (option A is wrong).

26:0.question: While reviewing a slow query in Snowsight's Query Profile, an engineer sees that an operator reports a high percentage of 'Bytes spilled to local storage' and 'Bytes spilled to remote storage'. What does this indicate?
options: The query result was too large to fit in the result cache and was discarded
The warehouse ran out of memory during processing and had to write intermediate data to disk (local) and then to remote storage, indicating the operation needs a larger warehouse
The micro-partitions being scanned were not pruned, causing a full table scan
The data was automatically clustered during the query, consuming extra credits
26:0.explanation: Spilling occurs when an operation cannot fit its intermediate results in memory, so it spills to local disk and, if that is exhausted, to remote storage—both of which degrade performance. The Query Profile surfaces these as 'Bytes spilled to local/remote storage', and the typical remedy is to use a larger warehouse (more memory) or reduce data processed. Result cache eviction (option A), lack of partition pruning (option C), and automatic clustering (option D) are unrelated to the spilling statistics shown in Query Profile.

24:1.question: An analyst notices that queries against an external table are much slower than expected and adding new files to the underlying cloud storage location does not immediately return in query results. Which statements about external tables are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: External table metadata must be refreshed (automatically or manually) so that Snowflake is aware of new or removed files in the stage
Query performance on external tables can be improved by defining partitions and creating a materialized view on the external table
External tables store the file data inside Snowflake's internal storage after the first query
The VALUE column of an external table represents each row from the external file in a VARIANT type
24:1.explanation: External table metadata records file-level metadata and must be refreshed—either automatically via event notifications or manually with ALTER EXTERNAL TABLE ... REFRESH—to reflect new or removed files (option A). Performance can be improved by partitioning the external table and by creating a materialized view over it (option B). Each row is exposed through the VALUE column as a VARIANT (option D). Option C is incorrect: external tables do not copy or store the file data inside Snowflake; the data remains in the external stage and only metadata is stored.

25:0.question: A team wants to maintain a queryable catalog of the files staged in a Snowflake stage, including each file's relative path, size, last-modified timestamp, and a scoped file URL. Which capability provides this?
options: A directory table enabled on the stage
The RESULT_SCAN function applied to a LIST command
An external table created over the stage
The INFORMATION_SCHEMA.STAGES view
25:0.explanation: A directory table is a built-in, read-only object layered on a stage that stores file-level metadata such as the relative file path, size, last-modified timestamp, and a file URL, making the staged files catalogable and queryable. RESULT_SCAN on LIST (option B) returns transient result data but is not a persistent catalog object. An external table (option C) exposes the contents of the files, not a metadata catalog of them. INFORMATION_SCHEMA.STAGES (option D) lists stage objects, not the files within a stage.

21:1.question: A developer creates a sequence and wants to use it to populate a primary key column each time a new row is inserted, without querying the sequence value in every INSERT statement. Which approach lets a table automatically draw the next value from a sequence?
options: Define the column with an IDENTITY constraint that references the sequence name directly in the constraint keyword
Set the sequence as the table's clustering key so new rows inherit incrementing values
Define the column's DEFAULT value as seq_name.NEXTVAL when creating the table
Enable AUTOINCREMENT on the sequence object so it writes to any table in the same schema
21:1.explanation: Correct: You reference a sequence in a column's DEFAULT expression using seq_name.NEXTVAL, so each inserted row automatically gets the next sequence value. Incorrect: IDENTITY/AUTOINCREMENT are column properties that generate values internally and are not tied to a named sequence via a constraint keyword. A clustering key controls data organization within micro-partitions, not value generation. AUTOINCREMENT is a column default option, not a sequence property that writes to tables in a schema.

19:1.question: A data engineer is loading JSON documents into a Snowflake table and must choose an appropriate semi-structured column type. Which statement accurately describes the maximum size of a VARIANT value in Snowflake?
options: A VARIANT value can hold a maximum of 16 MB of uncompressed data
A VARIANT value can hold a maximum of 128 MB of uncompressed data
A VARIANT value can hold a maximum of 128 MB of compressed data
A VARIANT value has no size limit as long as the micro-partition can store it
19:1.explanation: Snowflake documentation explicitly states that a VARIANT value can have a maximum size of up to 128 MB of uncompressed data. Option 1 is correct. Option 0 (16 MB) is a common misconception often confused with other limits. Option 2 is wrong because the limit is measured against uncompressed data, not the compressed representation. Option 3 is incorrect because there is a defined documented limit.

23:1.question: A development team wants to build a Python application that connects to Snowflake to run queries and load data. Which Snowflake connectivity option is specifically designed for Python applications?
options: Snowflake ODBC Driver
Snowflake JDBC Driver
Snowflake Connector for Python
Snowflake .NET Driver
23:1.explanation: The Snowflake Connector for Python is the Snowflake-provided driver designed for Python applications to connect to and interact with Snowflake. Option 2 is correct. Option 0 (ODBC Driver) is a general driver used by ODBC-compliant applications, not Python-specific. Option 1 (JDBC Driver) is designed for Java applications. Option 3 (.NET Driver) is designed for .NET applications. Only the Python connector targets Python specifically.

22:0.question: A developer needs to share access to an individual unstructured file stored in a Snowflake stage by generating a temporary link whose expiration period can be explicitly configured. Which URL type meets this requirement?
options: Scoped URL
File URL
Pre-signed URL
Directory URL
22:0.explanation: According to Snowflake's unstructured data documentation, a pre-signed URL is a simple HTTPS URL that provides access to a file without authentication, and its access token expiration time is configurable. Option 2 is correct. Option 0 (scoped URL) is wrong because a scoped URL's expiration is tied to the persisted query results cache period (currently 24 hours) and is not configurable. Option 1 (file URL) is a permanent URL requiring Snowflake authentication and authorization, with no expiration. Option 3 (directory URL) is not one of the defined unstructured file URL types.

26:1.question: An engineer is examining the Query Profile of a completed query in Snowsight. In the profile overview, they want to identify what fraction of the total execution time was spent on operations such as reading data from storage versus performing processing in the compute layer. Which Query Profile feature provides this breakdown of time spent across different categories of processing?
options: The 'Most Expensive Nodes' list, which ranks warehouses by credit consumption
The 'Profile Overview' with statistics such as Processing, Local Disk IO, Remote Disk IO, and Synchronization percentages
The 'Query History' retention setting, which controls how long profiles are stored
The 'Clustering Depth' metric, which reports the overlap of micro-partitions
26:1.explanation: The Query Profile Overview displays execution time broken into categories such as Processing (CPU), Local Disk IO, Remote Disk IO, and Synchronization, expressed as percentages of total time. Option 1 is correct. Option 0 is wrong because 'Most Expensive Nodes' ranks operator nodes by execution time, not warehouses by credits. Option 2 is wrong because Query History retention is unrelated to per-query time breakdowns. Option 3 (clustering depth) relates to table clustering quality, not query execution time categories.

20:1.question: A data scientist is defining a VECTOR column in Snowflake to store embeddings generated by an ML model. Which of the following is a correct statement about declaring and using the VECTOR data type?
options: A VECTOR column can be declared with element type VARCHAR to store text tokens directly
The VECTOR data type requires two parameters: the element type (INT or FLOAT) and the dimension (number of elements)
The dimension of a VECTOR column may be omitted, in which case Snowflake infers it from the first inserted value
A VECTOR can hold up to 65,536 elements regardless of the declared dimension
20:1.explanation: The VECTOR data type is declared as VECTOR(<element_type>, <dimension>), where the element type must be either INT or FLOAT and the dimension is a fixed number of elements — both parameters are required. Option A is wrong because VARCHAR is not a valid element type; only INT and FLOAT are supported. Option C is wrong because the dimension must be explicitly specified when declaring the column and is not inferred. Option D is wrong because the dimension is fixed by the declaration and each vector must match that declared dimension, not an arbitrary maximum.

27:0.question: In Snowflake's default system-defined role hierarchy, which role is the top-most role that is granted to (i.e., inherits the privileges of) both SYSADMIN and SECURITYADMIN?
options: USERADMIN
PUBLIC
ACCOUNTADMIN
ORGADMIN
27:0.explanation: ACCOUNTADMIN is the most powerful system-defined role and sits at the top of the role hierarchy. By default, both SYSADMIN and SECURITYADMIN are granted to ACCOUNTADMIN, so ACCOUNTADMIN inherits the privileges of both branches of the hierarchy. USERADMIN is incorrect because it is a child role dedicated to user and role management and is itself granted to SECURITYADMIN, not the top role. PUBLIC is incorrect because it is a pseudo-role automatically granted to every user and role and sits at the bottom of the hierarchy, not the top. ORGADMIN is incorrect because it manages operations at the organization level (such as creating and viewing accounts) and is not part of the account-level hierarchy under which SYSADMIN and SECURITYADMIN roll up; it is a separate role and not granted the privileges of SYSADMIN and SECURITYADMIN.

citations.title: Overview of Access Control — System-defined roles

28:0.question: Snowflake's access control model combines two frameworks: Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) and Discretionary Access Control (DAC). Which statement correctly describes the DAC aspect of Snowflake's model?
options: Privileges are assigned only to users directly, and roles are never used as an intermediary in the DAC model.
DAC requires the Enterprise Edition or higher, whereas RBAC is available in all editions.
Each securable object has an owner who can, in turn, grant access to that object to other roles.
DAC automatically grants every role in the account SELECT access to all newly created tables unless explicitly revoked.
28:0.explanation: Snowflake combines RBAC (privileges are assigned to roles, and roles are assigned to users) with DAC, in which each object has an owner who can grant access to that object. Option 2 is correct because under DAC each securable object has an owner, and that owner can grant access on the object to others. Option 0 is wrong because in Snowflake's RBAC model privileges are granted to roles rather than directly to users, and roles are the central mechanism, not something DAC avoids. Option 1 is wrong because the access control framework (both RBAC and DAC) is a core capability available across editions, not an Enterprise-only feature. Option 3 is wrong because Snowflake never automatically grants all roles access to new objects; access must be explicitly granted, and access is denied by default.

30:0.question: An administrator creates a network policy in Snowflake that specifies both an allowed IP address list and a blocked IP address list, and an IP address appears in both lists. According to Snowflake's evaluation rules for network policies, what is the result for a connection attempt from that IP address?
options: The connection is allowed, because the allowed list takes precedence over the blocked list
The connection is blocked, because Snowflake applies the blocked list first (the blocked list takes precedence)
The network policy fails validation and cannot be created with overlapping entries
The connection is allowed only if it also passes multi-factor authentication
30:0.explanation: Snowflake applies the blocked IP address list first when evaluating a network policy, so if an IP address appears in both the allowed and blocked lists, it is blocked.
